prompt:
You are a mathematical research advisor with access to web search. Your job is to orchestrate solver agents to assemble the best possible proof for the problem below. You have 10 advisor calls total (including this one). Plan strategically. Use web search whenever you need to look up specific results, verify claims, or find techniques.

# Problem
Let $G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle$ be a free group. A word on the
  letters $L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}$ is called reducible
  if it equals $1\in G$. A word is called quasi-reduced if it does not
  contain three consecutive letters $\ell\ell^{-1}\ell$ for any
  $\ell \in L$.

  Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word $w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}$ (with $w_i\in L$). A
  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in
  $\RR\times\RR_+$, satisfying the following:
  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of
    $\RR\times\RR_+\setminus M$, and for each component $A$ of $M\cap \bdy\overline{R}$, $\bdy A$ consists of some two points $(i,0)$ and $(j,0)$ with $w_i=w_j^{-1}$. 
  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,
  \]
  let $M_a$ be the $(k-1)$-crossing matching obtained by resolving $M$
  at $c$ according to $a$. Then the $(k-1)$-cell associated to $M_a$
  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

# Strategic Directions (from literature survey)
The problem is best viewed as a labeled uncrossing or rewriting cube complex. In dimension 0, the cells are exactly noncrossing inverse-letter foldings of the freely trivial word, equivalently valid plane trees; the deep-read material gives a strong normal form here: the greedy folding exists, the valid local-move graph is connected, and it has a unique sink/source depending on orientation. The hard part is not the existence of foldings, but extending this 0- and 1-dimensional structure to all crossing cells while respecting the region-label condition and isotopy classes of immersed diagrams.

## Directions to Explore

### Primary route: greedy-folding discrete Morse collapse
Try to prove that F_w collapses to the greedy noncrossing folding. First identify 0-cells with w-valid plane trees and 1-cells with the valid local moves of Condon-Heitsch-Hoos. Then extend the type-2 orientation toward the greedy tree to a discrete Morse matching on higher cubes: for each noncritical cell, choose the first local non-greedy feature and pair the cell with the cell obtained by adding or deleting the corresponding crossing coordinate. The target proof is collapsibility, hence contractibility, with the greedy folding as the only critical cell.
Key ideas: Use the greedy stack folding as the canonical base vertex.; Use the type-2 local-move orientation as a Lyapunov gradient on vertices.; For higher cells, use a leftmost or first-deviation rule analogous to Squier/Farley collapsing schemes.; Exploit quasi-reducedness to rule out overlapping cancellation ambiguities of the form ell ell^{-1} ell.
Relevant techniques: Valid Plane Trees: Combinatorial Models for RNA Secondary Structures with Watson-Crick Base Pairs, https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.03238, Proposition 2.6-2.7 gives the exact four-letter criterion for a valid local move; this should be the local model for every 1-cell of F_w.; Valid Plane Trees, https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.03238, Theorem 3.4 and Corollaries 3.5, 3.8, 3.10 give the greedy folding, unique sink, acyclicity, and directed paths to the greedy tree.; k-Foldability of Words, https://arxiv.org/abs/1710.10616, Basic equivalence in Section 2 says freely trivial words are precisely foldable words, so F_w has at least one 0-cell.; Diagram groups and directed 2-complexes: homotopy and homology, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0301225, Theorem 9.2 gives the leftmost-special-term collapsing scheme for Squier complexes; it is a good template for pairing higher-dimensional cells of F_w.; The cancellation norm and the geometry of bi-invariant word metrics, https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/BCA930D8BD51C5DCD2828E93216C40C9/S0017089515000129a.pdf/div-class-title-the-cancellation-norm-and-the-geometry-of-bi-invariant-word-metrics-div.pdf, Lemma 2.10 gives the first-letter inverse-occurrence split for free-group cancellations, useful for inductive control of the greedy target.
Potential obstacles: The main technical gap is extending the vertex orientation to cubes. A k-crossing matching may not be transparently a set of k independent valid tree rotations until one proves a precise local dictionary. One must also prove that the chosen first bad crossing always gives another admissible cell of F_w and that the resulting Morse matching is acyclic.

### Squier or Farley CAT(0) model for free cancellation
Build a directed 2-complex or Squier-type cube complex whose vertices are cancellation states or reduction diagrams for w, and whose cubes are simultaneous independent local cancellation moves. Then prove F_w is the universal cover or a convex subcomplex of that Squier complex. If successful, contractibility follows from Farley-type CAT(0) or universal-cover contractibility theorems.
Key ideas: Interpret a crossing as a pending binary choice between two local cancellation orders.; Use cubes for commuting independent cancellation choices.; Treat quasi-reducedness as the condition excluding overlapping reductions ell ell^{-1} ell.; Aim for a universal-cover model, not merely a quotient Squier complex, because ordinary Squier complexes need not be contractible.
Relevant techniques: Diagram groups and directed 2-complexes: homotopy and homology, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0301225, Theorem 8.5 proves the universal cover of a Squier complex is contractible.; Diagram groups and directed 2-complexes, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0301225, Lemma 3.7 realizes Squier cubes as positive paths through 2-cells; this closely matches the interval coordinates in the definition of C(M).; An introduction to diagram groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.12068, Theorem 4.9 proves that the diagram graph M(P,w) is median and its cube completion is CAT(0), hence contractible.; Groupoids and the Algebra of Rewriting in Group Presentations, https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.04348, Definition 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 show how group-presentation relation applications with disjoint contexts commute via Squier squares.; An introduction to diagram groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.12068, Section 5.3 caveat warns that monoid relations of the form u=1 can break nonpositive curvature; this is exactly why quasi-reducedness and a directed-2-complex rather than naive monoid presentation should be used.
Potential obstacles: Free cancellation uses empty-word relations, which are dangerous in ordinary semigroup Squier theory. Also, crossings in F_w are geometric and may correspond to nested rather than disjoint word contexts. The key test is whether every k-cell of F_w can be encoded as a thin diagram with k independent 2-cells and whether the region-label condition is exactly the Squier legality condition.

### Face-poset and shellability approach via uncrossing orders
Compare F_w with the full uncrossing poset of matchings. Prove first that every admissible crossing matching is lensless and that resolving crossings gives an interval or induced subcomplex inside the full uncrossing CW poset. Then try to show the label-compatible part is shellable or collapsible, possibly using a recursive atom/coatom order based on the greedy folding.
Key ideas: Use full uncrossing posets as the ambient regular CW model.; Characterize admissible cells as those whose complete resolutions are valid foldings of w.; Use the greedy folding to choose a recursive coatom order on the label-compatible subposet.; If the label-compatible subposet is an interval, order ideal, or shellable ball, contractibility follows.
Relevant techniques: Shellability of face posets of electrical networks and the CW poset property, https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.06217, Theorem 3.18 and Corollary 3.19 prove the full uncrossing poset is shellable and a CW poset.; Generalized Recursive Atom Ordering and Equivalence to CL-Shellability, https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.03949, Theorem 7.1 upgrades uncrossing posets to dual CL-shellable; its unique-earliest-label idea is a natural labeling strategy for admissible resolutions.; Circular Planar Electrical Networks I: The Electrical Poset EPn, https://arxiv.org/abs/1309.2697, Theorem 2.4.2 and Proposition 2.4.3 identify critical diagrams with lensless medial graphs and show how motions reduce dependence on drawings.; Electroid Varieties and a Compactification of the Space of Electrical Networks, https://arxiv.org/abs/1402.6261, Lemma 4.12 gives an endpoint criterion for when an uncrossing remains lensless.; The cd-indices of intervals in the uncrossing partial order on matchings, https://www.combinatorics.org/ojs/index.php/eljc/article/download/v26i1p40/pdf/, Theorem 10 and Theorem 28 give an affine-Bruhat encoding and product-plus-zipping interval recursion.; Subword Complexes in Coxeter Groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0309259, Theorems 2.5 and 3.7 give a model for proving a deletion-choice complex is vertex-decomposable and hence a ball or sphere.
Potential obstacles: The admissible label condition may not define an order ideal or interval in the full uncrossing poset. Also, the electrical-network literature often quotients lensless diagrams by Yang-Baxter motions, whereas F_w uses isotopy classes of actual immersed interval diagrams. Inherited shellability of an arbitrary induced subcomplex is not automatic.

### Hatcher-style surgery to the star of the greedy matching
Fix the greedy noncrossing matching T0. Put each crossing matching in minimal position relative to T0 and perform ordered surgeries that reduce intersections with T0, imitating Hatcher's contraction of arc complexes to the star of a fixed arc. The desired deformation retracts F_w to the cubical star of the greedy vertex.
Key ideas: Use T0 as the reference object.; Order intersections along arcs of T0 and remove them one at a time.; Make each surgery cellular by moving linearly inside a cube or adjacent cube.; Use interval decompositions along greedy arcs to reduce validity checks to smaller subwords.
Relevant techniques: Triangulations of Surfaces, https://pi.math.cornell.edu/~hatcher/Papers/TriangSurf.pdf, the surgery deformation retraction onto the star of a fixed arc is the direct geometric template.; Triangulations of Surfaces, https://pi.math.cornell.edu/~hatcher/Papers/TriangSurf.pdf, the minimal-position isotopy fact is relevant because operations on F_w must be well defined on isotopy classes.; Triangulations of Surfaces, https://pi.math.cornell.edu/~hatcher/Papers/TriangSurf.pdf, the link-after-cutting identification suggests how fixing a matched arc should split F_w into smaller word problems.; Valid Plane Trees, https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.03238, Proposition 2.8 says validity is inherited by interval subtrees after cutting along a common edge.
Potential obstacles: Surgery on arbitrary arc systems can discard boundary-parallel or duplicate arcs, but F_w consists of perfect matchings with fixed endpoints; careless surgery may leave the class of matchings. The star of a full matching vertex is also subtler than the star of a single arc, so the target subcomplex must be defined carefully.

### Longer-shot positive-geometry realization
Try to encode the letter labels as color or linear constraints on Lam's electrical-network compactification or its positive Grassmannian slice. If F_w can be realized as a cell closure, link, or positive slice preserved by a contractive flow, then existing ball theorems would imply contractibility, possibly even a closed-ball structure.
Key ideas: Use medial strands of electrical networks as the unlabeled version of the crossing matchings.; Translate inverse-letter compatibility into boundary-color constraints on medial regions or grove coordinates.; Seek a positive slice or cell closure whose face stratification is exactly F_w.; Apply contractive-flow or regularity theorems rather than constructing a combinatorial collapse.
Relevant techniques: Electroid Varieties and a Compactification of the Space of Electrical Networks, https://arxiv.org/abs/1402.6261, Propositions 4.10-4.11 and Theorem 5.8 give a compactification decomposed into cells indexed by matchings.; Electroid Varieties, https://arxiv.org/abs/1402.6261, Proposition 5.26 identifies closure order with uncrossing order.; The Totally Nonnegative Grassmannian is a Ball, https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.02010, Lemma 2.3 gives the contractive-flow ball criterion and Theorem 1.3 proves Lam's electrical compactification is a closed ball.; Regularity theorem for totally nonnegative flag varieties, https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.00527, Theorem 2.4 gives an abstract regularity criterion using shellability plus Fomin-Shapiro local product charts.
Potential obstacles: This is probably too heavy for a first proof. The known positive spaces index cells by endpoint matchings modulo electrical equivalence, while F_w has extra word labels and actual isotopy-class data. It is unclear that the label-compatible locus is semialgebraic, convex, positive, or flow-invariant.

## Key Obstacles
The central barrier is to find the right combinatorial model for a k-cell. The 0-cell theory is well understood through valid plane trees, and the 1-cell theory should be the valid local-move criterion, but contractibility depends on higher-dimensional compatibility of several crossings. One must prove that the region-label condition is stable under all face maps, that it excludes lens or overlap pathologies, and that quasi-reducedness is used exactly to rule out ambiguous overlapping cancellations. A second barrier is the quotient issue: uncrossing posets and electrical networks usually identify diagrams up to Yang-Baxter motions, while F_w is built from isotopy classes of immersed intervals. A third barrier is regularity: even if the face poset looks shellable, the attaching maps in F_w must be controlled well enough for poset topology or discrete Morse theory to apply.

## Recommended Starting Point
Start with the greedy-folding Morse route. The very first step should be a local dictionary lemma: prove that 0-cells of F_w are exactly w-valid plane trees, and that 1-cells of F_w are exactly the valid local moves characterized in Valid Plane Trees, Proposition 2.6-2.7, https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.03238. Immediately after that, prove the 2-cell compatibility lemma: two valid local moves span a square of F_w precisely when they can be performed independently, with the remaining three-edge cases matching the rank-two uncrossing analyses in Shellability of face posets of electrical networks, Lemma 4.1 and Proposition 4.3, https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.06217. If these two lemmas work cleanly, the discrete Morse collapse to the greedy folding is the most direct and problem-specific path to contractibility.

# Past Attempts on This Problem (from previous runs)


# Your Role
You are the sole orchestrator. You will:
1. Maintain a priority task list and a shared knowledge base.
2. Each round, assign 1–2 tasks to solver agents (parallel API calls).
3. Decide exactly what context each solver needs — you write their full prompts verbatim.
4. When you want a solver (or write-up agent) to read the **full text** of a prior    solver's output (e.g., to critique, attack, extend, or assemble it), put those task    IDs in `reference_task_ids` — the harness will auto-prepend the full output verbatim.    Do NOT try to paste prior outputs inline; just reference them by ID.
5. When significant partial results emerge, flag them for a dedicated write-up agent    (you write that prompt too, including all proof details found so far).

Note: you do NOT need to assemble a final solution yourself. When the budget runs out, a separate assembly stage will automatically synthesise the best possible solution from the shared KB. Focus every round on *exploration and proving new results*.

# Output Format
Reasoning prose first, then your plan inside an <ADVISOR_PLAN> tag. The content between the tags must be a single valid JSON object — do NOT wrap it in a markdown code block.

<ADVISOR_PLAN>

  "strategic_note": "2-3 sentence note for your future self: what you learned this round, what you decided, and why. This will be shown to you in every subsequent round as a memory aid.",
  "kb_updates": {

Use `reference_task_ids` to pull in any prior solver outputs you want this solver to read in full — do NOT paste them inline here."
    }
  ],
  "writeup_tasks": [

</ADVISOR_PLAN>

**reference_task_ids:** list of prior task IDs whose full outputs should be auto-attached to the agent's prompt. Strict for solver tasks (unknown IDs cause the task to fail) and lenient for writeups (unknown IDs are skipped with a warning). On round 1 there are no prior tasks, so leave this empty.

**solves_original_problem (writeup_tasks only):** set to `true` ONLY when the `statement` is the full original problem (not a relaxation, not a lemma, not a partial). When `true` and the resulting write-up passes verify+refine, the assembly stage will reuse the verified write-up verbatim instead of spending a fresh solver call. Set to `false` for any partial result, lemma, or relaxation. This flag is also used by the verifier to pick its framing: `true` ⇒ verify against the original problem verbatim; `false` ⇒ verify against the write-up's stated claim. Setting it incorrectly will give you a misleading verification verdict, so be honest about what is actually being written up.

**writeup_agent_prompt (writeup_tasks only):** the write-up agent will be evaluated by an independent referee, and the resulting artifact then flows downstream into benchmark and LaTeX-typesetting stages, none of which have access to this conversation, the shared KB, the solver outputs, or any custom notation introduced along the way. Your prompt MUST therefore instruct the agent to produce a **fully self-contained artifact**: (1) state the exact claim being proved up front, with every symbol, hypothesis, quantifier, and notational convention defined inline; (2) write the proof so a reader who has never seen the solver history, the KB, or this conversation can follow it end-to-end; (3) avoid any reference to "the problem above", "the previous result", "the construction we used", or other shorthand that depends on context the referee cannot see. If the agent needs concrete prior outputs to build on, list them in `reference_task_ids` (which auto-attaches them verbatim) — do NOT assume inherited context.

**action values:** `"continue"` — keep exploring; `"done"` — no more work needed.

Assign at most 2 solver tasks this round. Start with the highest-priority direction.

You are Solver B. Your job is to test the statement by small examples and look for counterexamples. Do not assume contractibility.

Problem. Let G=<a_1,...,a_r> be free, and let w=w_1...w_{2n} be a reducible quasi-reduced word. F_w is the CW complex whose k-cells are isotopy classes of k-crossing matchings of the endpoints 1,...,2n in the upper half-plane satisfying the region-label rule: for every complementary region R and every component A of M cap boundary(closure R), the two boundary endpoints of A are i,j with w_i=w_j^{-1}. Cell facets are obtained by resolving one crossing in either way. Is F_w contractible?

Important: in a crossing cell, the endpoints of an individual interval need not themselves have inverse labels; the inverse-label condition is imposed on region-side boundary components.

Tasks:
1. Establish a practical combinatorial model for small cases. Does the region-label condition forbid lenses and force any two intervals to cross at most once? If yes, explain; if no, draw or describe a counterexample. Decide whether an isotopy class can be represented by an ordinary chord diagram with a crossing set determined by endpoint interleaving.
2. Work out in detail the separated alternating skeleton with three positive a's and three negative a's. To make it quasi-reduced, use for instance a word of the form a b b^{-1} a^{-1} c c^{-1} a d d^{-1} a^{-1} e e^{-1} a f f^{-1} a^{-1}, where b,c,d,e,f are distinct from a and each other. The filler pairs should be forced and uncrossed. Enumerate the cells coming from the six a/a^{-1} skeleton endpoints. Verify the expected five 0-cells, six 1-cells, and determine exactly which 2-cells occur. Compute the homotopy type in this example.
3. Search manually or by giving precise pseudocode/Python for all quasi-reduced reducible words of small length, say up to length 8 or 10 over two generators, modulo relabeling/inversion, and compute the cellular chain complex of F_w. The priority is to find a noncontractible example: disconnected F_w, nonzero H_1, or a cycle of valid 1-cells not filled by valid 2-cells. If none appears, state the evidence and what structural pattern seems to force fillings.
4. Specifically test the missing-square phenomenon: can four valid 1-cells form the boundary of a square combinatorially while the corresponding 2-crossing matching violates the region-label condition? If yes, this is likely a counterexample mechanism; if no, formulate the compatibility lemma suggested by the small cases.

Deliverable: either a concrete counterexample with full cell enumeration and homology, or a detailed small-case report supporting contractibility plus the key compatibility lemmas suggested by the enumeration.

---

response:
## Short answer: no

Using the separated alternating skeleton requested in Task 2, \(F_w\) is **not contractible**. In fact, for that word the forced filler pairs reduce the problem to six alternating \(a/a^{-1}\) endpoints, and the resulting CW complex is a **2-sphere**.

---

## 1. Practical combinatorial model

A valid cell can be enumerated by an ordinary chord matching, with one important extra test.

### No lenses / no repeated crossings

Yes: the region-label condition forbids lenses and forces any two intervals to cross at most once.

Reason: if two intervals cross twice, the two subarcs between the crossings form an interior Jordan curve. Since all endpoints lie on the boundary line, some complementary region inside that curve has no boundary endpoint on its \(M\)-boundary component. That violates the rule requiring every such component to have two boundary endpoints \(i,j\) with \(w_i=w_j^{-1}\).

More generally, the graph formed by the intervals and crossings cannot contain an interior cycle. Thus:

- no self-crossing intervals;
- no two intervals cross more than once;
- after isotopy, a cell is represented by an ordinary chord diagram;
- two intervals cross exactly when their endpoints interleave.

### Validity criterion for a chord matching

For a perfect matching \(P\), form its crossing graph \(\Gamma(P)\):

- vertices are intervals/chords;
- two vertices are adjacent if the corresponding chord endpoints interleave.

Then \(P\) represents a valid cell iff:

1. \(\Gamma(P)\) is a forest;
2. for each connected component \(C\) of \(\Gamma(P)\), list all endpoints belonging to chords in \(C\) in boundary order:
   \[
   s_1<s_2<\cdots<s_{2m}.
   \]
   Then require cyclically
   \[
   w_{s_t}=w_{s_{t+1}}^{-1}
   \]
   for all \(t\), with \(s_{2m+1}=s_1\).

This also explains why endpoints of an individual crossing interval need not be inverse: in a crossing component, the inverse-pair test is on consecutive endpoints around the component, not on the endpoints of each interval.

---

## 2. The separated alternating skeleton with three \(a\)’s and three \(a^{-1}\)’s

Use the word from the prompt:
\[
w=
a\, b\, b^{-1}\, a^{-1}\,
c\, c^{-1}\,
a\, d\, d^{-1}\, a^{-1}\,
e\, e^{-1}\,
a\, f\, f^{-1}\, a^{-1}.
\]

The six \(a/a^{-1}\) skeleton endpoints are:
\[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16,
\]
with signs
\[
+,-,+,-,+,-.
\]

The filler pairs \((2,3),(5,6),(8,9),(11,12),(14,15)\) are forced: each filler generator occurs exactly once positively and once negatively, so in any valid component each filler endpoint would need both cyclic neighbors to be its unique inverse. That is possible only in a two-endpoint isolated component. Since each filler pair is adjacent, it is uncrossed.

So \(F_w\) is exactly the six-endpoint alternating skeleton complex, with five fixed filler arcs added.

Renumber the skeleton endpoints as \(1,2,3,4,5,6\) with labels \(+,-,+,-,+,-\).

### 0-cells

Write \(ij\) for the chord joining endpoints \(i,j\).

The five valid 0-cells are:

| vertex | matching |
|---|---|
| \(v_1\) | \(12\mid34\mid56\) |
| \(v_2\) | \(12\mid36\mid45\) |
| \(v_3\) | \(14\mid23\mid56\) |
| \(v_4\) | \(16\mid23\mid45\) |
| \(v_5\) | \(16\mid25\mid34\) |

These are exactly the five Catalan noncrossing matchings.

### 1-cells

A one-crossing component on endpoints \(i<j<k<l\) is valid exactly when the four labels alternate cyclically. The six valid 1-cells are:

| edge | matching | boundary vertices |
|---|---|---|
| \(e_1\) | \(13\times24\mid56\) | \(v_1,v_3\) |
| \(e_2\) | \(13\times26\mid45\) | \(v_2,v_4\) |
| \(e_3\) | \(15\times26\mid34\) | \(v_1,v_5\) |
| \(e_4\) | \(15\times46\mid23\) | \(v_3,v_4\) |
| \(e_5\) | \(24\times35\mid16\) | \(v_4,v_5\) |
| \(e_6\) | \(35\times46\mid12\) | \(v_1,v_2\) |

The 1-skeleton is \(K_{2,3}\), with poles \(v_1,v_4\) and middle vertices \(v_2,v_3,v_5\).

### 2-cells

There are exactly three two-crossing forest matchings on six endpoints:

| 2-cell | matching | square boundary |
|---|---|---|
| \(f_1\) | \(13\mid25\mid46\) | \(v_1 \xrightarrow{e_6} v_2 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_4 \xrightarrow{e_4} v_3 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_1\) |
| \(f_2\) | \(14\mid26\mid35\) | \(v_1 \xrightarrow{e_6} v_2 \xrightarrow{e_2} v_4 \xrightarrow{e_5} v_5 \xrightarrow{e_3} v_1\) |
| \(f_3\) | \(15\mid24\mid36\) | \(v_1 \xrightarrow{e_1} v_3 \xrightarrow{e_4} v_4 \xrightarrow{e_5} v_5 \xrightarrow{e_3} v_1\) |

Each is valid because its crossing graph is a path, and the cyclic endpoint order is \(+,-,+,-,+,-\).

There is no valid 3-cell. The only all-three-crossing matching is
\[
14\mid25\mid36,
\]
whose crossing graph is a triangle. The chord diagram has a central triangular complementary region with no boundary endpoints, violating the region-label rule.

### Homotopy type

The CW data are:

\[
\#C_0=5,\qquad \#C_1=6,\qquad \#C_2=3.
\]

The 1-skeleton has cycle rank
\[
6-5+1=2.
\]

The three square boundaries span a rank-2 subgroup of \(C_1\), with one relation among them. Thus

\[
H_0(F_w)\cong \mathbb Z,\qquad
H_1(F_w)=0,\qquad
H_2(F_w)\cong \mathbb Z.
\]

Equivalently, the complex is the standard three-square cellulation of \(S^2\). Therefore:

\[
F_w\simeq S^2,
\]

so \(F_w\) is **not contractible**.

---

## 3. Python search model for small words

Here is precise Python implementing the chord-diagram criterion above. Labels are integers: \(1\) means \(a\), \(-1\) means \(a^{-1}\), \(2\) means \(b\), etc.

```python
from itertools import combinations, product
from collections import defaultdict

def norm_pair(p):
    a, b = p
    return (a, b) if a < b else (b, a)

def norm_matching(pairs):
    return tuple(sorted(norm_pair(p) for p in pairs))

def pairings(items):
    items = tuple(items)
    if not items:
        yield ()
        return
    a = items[0]
    for t in range(1, len(items)):
        b = items[t]
        rest = items[1:t] + items[t+1:]
        for M in pairings(rest):
            yield norm_matching(((a, b),) + M)

def interleave(p, q):
    a, b = norm_pair(p)
    c, d = norm_pair(q)
    return (a < c < b < d) or (c < a < d < b)

class DSU:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.p = list(range(n))

    def find(self, x):
        while self.p[x] != x:
            self.p[x] = self.p[self.p[x]]
            x = self.p[x]
        return x

    def union(self, a, b):
        ra, rb = self.find(a), self.find(b)
        if ra == rb:
            return False
        self.p[rb] = ra
        return True

def valid_dim(labels, M):
    """
    Return crossing number/dimension if M is valid, else None.
    labels are 0-indexed integers, inverse given by negation.
    """
    m = len(M)
    dsu = DSU(m)
    k = 0

    # Build crossing graph and reject cycles.
    for i, j in combinations(range(m), 2):
        if interleave(M[i], M[j]):
            k += 1
            if not dsu.union(i, j):
                return None

    comps = defaultdict(list)
    for i in range(m):
        comps[dsu.find(i)].append(i)

    # Region-label rule: endpoints in each crossing-tree component
    # must alternate inverse labels cyclically.
    for comp in comps.values():
        ends = sorted(e for idx in comp for e in M[idx])
        cyc = ends[1:] + ends[:1]
        for u, v in zip(ends, cyc):
            if labels[u] != -labels[v]:
                return None

    return k

def cells_by_dim(labels):
    D = defaultdict(list)
    for M in pairings(range(len(labels))):
        d = valid_dim(labels, M)
        if d is not None:
            D[d].append(M)
    return {d: sorted(v) for d, v in D.items()}

def resolutions_at(M, i, j):
    xs = sorted(M[i] + M[j])
    a, b, c, d = xs
    rest = [M[t] for t in range(len(M)) if t not in (i, j)]
    return (
        norm_matching(rest + [(a, b), (c, d)]),
        norm_matching(rest + [(a, d), (b, c)]),
    )

def boundary_matrix_mod2(cells, d):
    cols = cells.get(d, [])
    rows = cells.get(d - 1, [])
    rind = {M: i for i, M in enumerate(rows)}
    A = [[0] * len(cols) for _ in rows]

    for col, M in enumerate(cols):
        for i, j in combinations(range(len(M)), 2):
            if interleave(M[i], M[j]):
                for F in resolutions_at(M, i, j):
                    if F not in rind:
                        raise RuntimeError(f"Missing facet: {M} -> {F}")
                    A[rind[F]][col] ^= 1
    return A

def rank_mod2(A):
    A = [row[:] for row in A]
    if not A:
        return 0
    m, n = len(A), len(A[0])
    r = 0
    for c in range(n):
        pivot = None
        for i in range(r, m):
            if A[i][c]:
                pivot = i
                break
        if pivot is None:
            continue
        A[r], A[pivot] = A[pivot], A[r]
        for i in range(m):
            if i != r and A[i][c]:
                for j in range(c, n):
                    A[i][j] ^= A[r][j]
        r += 1
    return r

def betti_mod2(cells):
    maxd = max(cells) if cells else -1
    ranks = {}
    for d in range(1, maxd + 1):
        ranks[d] = rank_mod2(boundary_matrix_mod2(cells, d))

    betti = []
    for d in range(maxd + 1):
        cd = len(cells.get(d, []))
        rd = ranks.get(d, 0)
        rnext = ranks.get(d + 1, 0)
        betti.append(cd - rd - rnext)
    return betti

# Skeleton sanity check: + - + - + -
labels = (1, -1, 1, -1, 1, -1)
cells = cells_by_dim(labels)
print({d: len(cells[d]) for d in sorted(cells)})
print(betti_mod2(cells))
# Expected:
# {0: 5, 1: 6, 2: 3}
# [1, 0, 1]

# Full separated word from the prompt:
full_labels = (
    1, 2, -2, -1,
    3, -3,
    1, 4, -4, -1,
    5, -5,
    1, 6, -6, -1
)
full_cells = cells_by_dim(full_labels)
print({d: len(full_cells[d]) for d in sorted(full_cells)})
print(betti_mod2(full_cells))
# Expected:
# {0: 5, 1: 6, 2: 3}
# [1, 0, 1]
```

```python
def has_inverse_pair(w):
    S = set(w)
    return any(-x in S for x in S)

def transform_two_gen(w, swap=False, s1=1, s2=1):
    out = []
    for x in w:
        sign = 1 if x > 0 else -1
        g = abs(x)
        newg = 3 - g if swap else g
        flip = s1 if g == 1 else s2
        out.append(sign * flip * newg)
    return tuple(out)

def canonical_two_gen(w, include_reversal=True):
    base_words = [tuple(w)]
    if include_reversal:
        base_words.append(tuple(reversed(w)))

    cands = []
    for ww in base_words:
        for swap in (False, True):
            for s1 in (1, -1):
                for s2 in (1, -1):
                    cands.append(transform_two_gen(ww, swap, s1, s2))
    return min(cands)

def scan_two_gen(max_len=10, quasi=lambda w: True):
    letters = (1, -1, 2, -2)
    seen = set()

    for N in range(2, max_len + 1, 2):
        for w in product(letters, repeat=N):
            if not has_inverse_pair(w):
                continue
            if not quasi(w):
                continue

            cw = canonical_two_gen(w)
            if cw in seen:
                continue
            seen.add(cw)

            cells = cells_by_dim(w)
            if not cells.get(0):
                continue

            b = betti_mod2(cells)
            if b[0] != 1 or any(x != 0 for x in b[1:]):
                print(
                    "word =", cw,
                    "counts =", {d: len(cells[d]) for d in sorted(cells)},
                    "betti_F2 =", b
                )

# Example quasi-reduced filter can be inserted here if desired.
# The prompt's separated example uses adjacent filler inverse pairs,
# so the default accepts all words.
scan_two_gen(max_len=10)
```

The main counterexample above has nonzero \(H_2\), not nonzero \(H_1\). So a search looking only for unfilled 1-cycles would miss the obstruction.

---

## 4. Missing-square phenomenon

In the ordinary chord-diagram model, I do **not** find missing squares.

The relevant compatibility lemma is:

> If a two-crossing ordinary chord matching has crossing graph a forest, and all four one-crossing resolutions are valid, then the two-crossing matching itself is valid.

Sketch: with two crossings, the crossing graph is either two disjoint edges or a length-two path. In the disjoint case, each crossing component is already tested in a facet. In the path case, the six boundary endpoints of the tree must occur in cyclic order, and every adjacent pair in that cyclic order appears as a region-side pair in one of the four one-crossing facets. Thus the inverse-label condition on the facets forces the inverse-label condition on the 2-cell.

So the counterexample mechanism here is **not** a missing square. It is the opposite: all three possible squares occur and kill \(H_1\), but the would-be higher crossing configuration has a cyclic crossing graph and is forbidden, leaving an \(S^2\).

Problem. Let G=<a_1,...,a_r> be a free group and let L={a_1,a_1^{-1},...,a_r,a_r^{-1}}. A word on L is reducible if it equals 1 in G. It is quasi-reduced if it contains no three consecutive letters ell ell^{-1} ell. Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word w=w_1...w_{2n}. A matching M consists of n properly embedded intervals in the upper half-plane with boundary endpoints 1,...,2n on the real line, with transverse pairwise crossings and no triple crossings. For every complementary region R and every component A of M cap boundary(closure R), the two boundary endpoints of A are some i,j with w_i=w_j^{-1}. A k-crossing matching has k total crossings. The CW complex F_w has one k-cell C(M)=product over crossings c of intervals I_c for each isotopy class of k-crossing matching, with facets obtained by resolving one crossing in either of the two local ways. Question: is F_w contractible?

Allowed black boxes, if you state them clearly: freely trivial words admit noncrossing inverse-letter foldings; valid plane trees for w have a greedy folding; the type-2 valid local-move orientation on valid plane trees is acyclic and has the greedy folding as unique sink. response:
## Conclusion

**No, \(F_w\) is not always contractible.**  
I’ll first prove the requested \(0\)- and \(1\)-cell dictionary and the facet-admissibility fact, then give a quasi-reduced counterexample whose \(F_w\) has \(H_2\cong \mathbb Z\).

Throughout, a **side** of a matching means a connected component of  
\[
\Gamma(M)\cap \partial\overline R
\]
where \(\Gamma(M)\) is the planar graph obtained from \(M\) by making crossings into vertices, and \(R\) is a complementary region.

---

## 1. Basic topology and the \(0\)-/\(1\)-cell dictionary

### Lemma 1: admissible matching shadows are forests

If \(M\) satisfies the region-label condition, then \(\Gamma(M)\) has no graph cycle.

**Proof.** A cycle in \(\Gamma(M)\) cannot pass through a boundary endpoint, since boundary endpoints have degree \(1\). Hence it is a Jordan curve in the open upper half-plane. Its interior contains at least one complementary region \(R\) whose boundary is entirely in \(\Gamma(M)\), so some component of \(\Gamma(M)\cap \partial\overline R\) has no two real-line boundary endpoints. This contradicts the definition of admissibility. ∎

In particular, two intervals cannot cross twice, and the interval-crossing graph is a forest.

---

### Lemma 2: \(0\)-cells are exactly noncrossing inverse-letter foldings

A \(0\)-crossing matching is just a noncrossing perfect matching of the positions \(1,\dots,2n\). Each arc is a side of adjacent complementary regions. Therefore the region-label condition is exactly:
\[
\text{if } i \text{ is paired with } j,\quad w_i=w_j^{-1}.
\]
So the \(0\)-cells of \(F_w\) are precisely the noncrossing inverse-letter foldings of \(w\). ∎

---

### Lemma 3: the exact \(1\)-cell criterion

Let \(M\) be a \(1\)-crossing matching, and suppose the crossing intervals have endpoints
\[
i<j<k<\ell.
\]
Then the crossing intervals are necessarily \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\). Around the crossing, the four side paths have endpoint pairs
\[
(i,j),\qquad (j,k),\qquad (k,\ell),\qquad (i,\ell).
\]
Thus the region-label condition at the crossing is exactly
\[
w_i=w_j^{-1},\quad
w_j=w_k^{-1},\quad
w_k=w_\ell^{-1},\quad
w_i=w_\ell^{-1}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
w_i=w_k=x,\qquad w_j=w_\ell=x^{-1}
\]
for some letter \(x\), up to replacing \(x\) by \(x^{-1}\). In words, the four labels alternate:
\[
x,\ x^{-1},\ x,\ x^{-1}.
\]

The two facets of this \(1\)-cell are the two local smoothings:
\[
(i,j),(k,\ell)
\]
and
\[
(i,\ell),(j,k).
\]
Both are noncrossing inverse-letter foldings by the criterion above. ∎

---

## 2. Facets and the weak \(k\)-cell dictionary

### Lemma 4: every facet of an admissible cell is admissible

Let \(M\) be an admissible \(k\)-crossing matching, and let \(c\) be one crossing. Resolving \(c\) in either local way gives an admissible \((k-1)\)-crossing matching.

**Proof.** Work inside a small disk around \(c\). The four local quadrants give four side-turns. A smoothing chooses two opposite side-turns and replaces the crossing by two small embedded arcs following exactly those turns.

Every side of the smoothed diagram is either:

1. a side of \(M\) disjoint from \(c\), or  
2. a side of \(M\) passing through \(c\), with \(c\) rounded according to the chosen smoothing.

No new side is formed by concatenating unrelated old sides. Hence every new side has the same pair of boundary endpoints as some old side of \(M\). Since \(M\) was admissible, those endpoints carry inverse letters. ∎

Therefore every partial smoothing of an admissible \(k\)-cell is admissible, and every full smoothing is a \(0\)-cell, i.e. a noncrossing inverse-letter folding.

So a \(k\)-cell gives a cube of simultaneous local type-2 moves among foldings. But this compatibility is **not** the same as disjoint support: two crossings may share an interval and still form a square. The obstruction below comes exactly from such overlapping squares.

---

## 3. A useful forcing lemma

### Lemma 5: a unique inverse pair is forced

Suppose a generator \(b\) occurs exactly once as \(b\) and exactly once as \(b^{-1}\) in \(w\), say at positions \(p,q\). Then in every admissible matching, \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by an uncrossed interval.

**Proof.** At the boundary endpoint \(p\), there are two incident sides. By admissibility, both must end at the unique endpoint \(q\) labelled \(b^{-1}\).

If the interval incident to \(p\) had a first crossing, then the two sides leaving \(p\) would diverge at that crossing, giving two distinct graph paths from \(p\) to \(q\). But \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest by Lemma 1, so there is a unique path between two vertices. Contradiction.

Thus the interval from \(p\) has no crossings, and admissibility forces its other endpoint to be \(q\). ∎

If \(p,q\) are adjacent positions, this interval is boundary-parallel and completely forced.

---

## 4. A quasi-reduced counterexample

Let
\[
G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle
\]
and write inverses by uppercase letters:
\[
A=a^{-1},\ B=b^{-1},\ \dots,\ F=f^{-1}.
\]

Consider the word
\[
w
=
a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]

It freely reduces as
\[
a\,bB\,A\,cC\,a\,dD\,A\,eE\,a\,fF\,A
\longrightarrow
aA\,aA\,aA
\longrightarrow 1.
\]
So \(w\) is reducible.

It is also quasi-reduced. The only adjacent inverse pairs among the separator letters are
\[
bB,\ cC,\ dD,\ eE,\ fF,
\]
and none is followed by the same letter again. Hence no subword has the form
\[
\ell\,\ell^{-1}\,\ell.
\]

Now \(b,c,d,e,f\) each occur exactly once together with their inverses. By Lemma 5, the adjacent pairs
\[
(2,3),\ (5,6),\ (8,9),\ (11,12),\ (14,15)
\]
are forced uncrossed arcs in every cell of \(F_w\).

Deleting these forced arcs leaves the six-letter word
\[
u=a\,A\,a\,A\,a\,A.
\]
Conversely, every admissible matching for \(u\) extends uniquely to one for \(w\) by adding the five forced boundary-parallel arcs. Therefore
\[
F_w\cong F_u.
\]

So it remains to compute \(F_u\).

---

## 5. The complex \(F_u\) for \(u=aAaAaA\)

Write a folding by listing paired positions. The \(0\)-cells are the five noncrossing inverse-letter matchings:

\[
\begin{aligned}
v_0&=(12)(34)(56),\\
v_1&=(14)(23)(56),\\
v_2&=(12)(36)(45),\\
v_3&=(16)(23)(45),\\
v_4&=(16)(25)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]

The \(1\)-cells are exactly:

\[
v_0v_1,\quad
v_0v_2,\quad
v_0v_4,\quad
v_1v_3,\quad
v_2v_3,\quad
v_4v_3.
\]

The admissible \(2\)-cells are exactly the following three squares:

\[
Q_1:\quad v_0\to v_1\to v_3\to v_2\to v_0,
\]
coming from the crossing matching
\[
(13)(25)(46);
\]

\[
Q_2:\quad v_0\to v_2\to v_3\to v_4\to v_0,
\]
coming from
\[
(14)(26)(35);
\]

\[
Q_3:\quad v_0\to v_1\to v_3\to v_4\to v_0,
\]
coming from
\[
(15)(24)(36).
\]

There are no higher cells: by Lemma 1, the interval-crossing graph of any admissible matching is a forest, and with only three intervals it can have at most two crossings.

Thus \(F_u\) has

\[
5\text{ vertices},\quad 6\text{ edges},\quad 3\text{ squares}.
\]

Orient the edges
\[
e_i:v_0\to v_i\quad (i=1,2,4),
\]
and
\[
f_i:v_i\to v_3\quad (i=1,2,4).
\]
Then the square boundaries are

\[
\partial Q_1=e_1+f_1-f_2-e_2,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=e_2+f_2-f_4-e_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=e_1+f_1-f_4-e_4.
\]

So
\[
\partial Q_3=\partial Q_1+\partial Q_2.
\]
The boundary map \(C_2\to C_1\) has rank \(2\), hence
\[
H_2(F_u)\cong \mathbb Z.
\]

Therefore
\[
H_2(F_w)\cong H_2(F_u)\cong \mathbb Z,
\]
so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

---

## 6. Where quasi-reducedness fails to help

The unpadded word
\[
aAaAaA
\]
is not quasi-reduced because it contains \(aAa\). But inserting forced adjacent inverse pairs between the \(a/A\)-letters produces the quasi-reduced word \(w\) above without changing the complex.

Thus quasi-reducedness only rules out immediate adjacent overlap ambiguities. It does **not** rule out the same overlap phenomenon after forced cancellations. The proposed greedy Morse collapse cannot exist in general, because this quasi-reduced example has nonzero \(H_2\).

  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,

  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Main Theorem (contractibility of arc and curve-system complexes)] Let S be a compact connected surface, possibly with boundary, and let V be a nonempty finite subset of S. An essential arc in (S,V) means an embedded arc meeting ∂S ∪ V only in its endpoints, whose endpoints lie in V and may coincide, and which does not separate S into two components one of which is a disk meeting V only in the endpoints of the arc. An essential circle in (S,V) means an embedded circle in S disjoint from ∂S ∪ V which does not separate S into two components one of which is either a disk meeting V in at most one point or an annulus disjoint from V. Let A(S,V) be the simplicial complex whose k-simplices are ambient-isotopy classes rel V of systems of k+1 pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs, disjoint except possibly at endpoints. Let C(S,V) be the analogous complex of systems of pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs and essential circles, mutually disjoint except possibly for arc endpoints. Excluding the trivial cases in which the complex under discussion is empty: (a) A(S,V) is contractible except when S is a disk with V contained in ∂S or when S is an annulus with V contained in one component of ∂S; in these exceptional cases A(S,V) is homeomorphic to a sphere. (b) If V contains at least one point in the interior of S, then C(S,V) is contractible.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: First prove (a) when V has at most one point on each boundary component. Choose an essential arc β. The surgery construction stated separately below gives a deformation retraction of A(S,V) onto the closed star of [β], the subcomplex of arc systems disjoint from β; a star is contractible. For general V, use the proposition that if V′ is obtained from V by adding one new vertex on a boundary component already containing a vertex, then A(S,V′) is homeomorphic to the suspension ΣA(S,V). The proof of this proposition compares both spaces by decomposing A(S,V′) into the subcomplex X of systems not containing either of two boundary-parallel arcs β,β′ adjacent to the new vertex, plus the two stars St(β), St(β′). Collapsing the boundary segment between the old and new vertex sends X into ΣA(S,V); the suspension coordinate is (θ−θ′)/2, where θ and θ′ are the total weights of arcs incident to the two adjacent vertices. The two complementary open cones are precisely the stars of β and β′, giving a PL homeomorphism. Inductively remove extra boundary vertices until each boundary component has at most one vertex. Suspension preserves contractibility, while repeated suspension of the initial S^0 cases gives spheres. The initial S^0 exceptional cases are the quadrilateral disk and the annulus with two vertices on one boundary component, yielding exactly the stated disk and annulus exceptions after adding boundary vertices. For (b), if V has an interior point, choose an essential arc β with both endpoints at that interior vertex; the same surgery deformation works for curve systems containing circles as well as arcs and retracts C(S,V) to St([β]), hence C(S,V) is contractible.
  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Surgery deformation to the star of a fixed arc (construction in the proof of the Main Theorem)] Let S be a compact connected surface and V a nonempty finite subset. Let β be an essential arc in (S,V). In the arc-only case, assume V has at most one point in each component of ∂S and set X = A(S,V). In the curve-system case, assume β has both endpoints at a point of V lying in the interior of S and set X = C(S,V). Then X admits a deformation retraction, indeed a flow which is linear on simplices in the sense described below, onto the closed star St_X([β]). This star consists of all simplices represented by curve systems disjoint from β, allowing one member of the system to be isotopic to β. The deformation fixes St_X([β]) pointwise.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: For a simplex [α0,...,αk], isotope the representing system c1 = α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk into minimal position with β: there is no disk whose boundary is an arc in β and an arc in some αi, not equal to αi ∪ β, with interior disjoint from V. The construction is independent of the chosen minimal representative because any two minimal positions are isotopic through minimal positions: take an isotopy F from one to the other, perturb F transverse to β, then simplify the 1-manifold F^{-1}(β) by cancelling critical points of its projection to the isotopy parameter. View a point P = Σ ti αi of the simplex as a weighted curve system by replacing αi with parallel copies of total thickness ti. At the crossings with β, pinch these families into one band of total thickness θ. Choose an end of β. For t ∈ [0,1], cut into this band from the chosen end through thickness tθ and redirect the two cut ends along β to that end. Discard arcs which become inessential, in particular boundary-parallel arcs as in the paper's Figure 3; in the arc-only case, the hypothesis of at most one vertex on each boundary component ensures that not all of the weighted system is discarded. Equivalently and more combinatorially, order the intersection points x1,...,xm of c1 with β from the chosen end. Slide x1 to the end of β, then discard inessential or parallel pieces, obtaining a new curve system c2 meeting β in x2,...,xm. The union c1 ∪ c2 is again a curve system and determines a simplex σ1 containing the original simplex as a face; the initial part of the deformation moves linearly along fibers of the linear projection of σ1 to the face corresponding to c2. Repeat, sliding x2, then x3, and so on, producing c3,...,cm+1 with cm+1 disjoint from β. Thus each starting simplex is crossed by a finite sequence of linear motions through simplices σ1,...,σm. The endpoint lies in St([β]). If the starting system is already disjoint from β, there are no intersections and the flow is stationary. The construction on a face is exactly the restriction of the construction on the containing simplex, since it uses the same ordered intersections with weights of omitted curves set to zero. Hence the simplexwise flows glue to a continuous global deformation retraction.
  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Cutting-open/link identification (used in the proof of the Corollary on elementary moves)] Let τ = [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V), represented by a system of essential arcs in a compact connected surface S with finite vertex set V. Let (S_τ,V_τ) be the possibly disconnected surface with vertices obtained by cutting S open along representatives of the arcs α0,...,αk; for disconnected surfaces, define A(S_τ,V_τ) to be the join of the arc complexes of the connected components. Then the link of τ in A(S,V) is naturally identified with A(S_τ,V_τ). Consequently, using the Main Theorem, whenever this cut-open arc complex has dimension at least one it is connected; in particular, in the corollary's application, links of simplices of codimension at least two are connected.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: A simplex in the link of τ is represented by an arc system γ disjoint from α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk and containing no arc isotopic rel V to any αi. Cutting S along the αi turns γ into an arc system in S_τ. Conversely, any essential arc system in S_τ glues to an arc system in S disjoint from the αi. The essentialness conditions match: an arc in the cut surface that cut off a disk with only its endpoints as vertices would glue to an inessential arc in S, while arcs parallel to the new boundary pieces created by cutting correspond precisely to arcs isotopic to one of the αi and hence are excluded from the link. Ambient isotopies rel vertices disjoint from the αi lift to isotopies in S_τ, and isotopies in S_τ descend after gluing, so the correspondence is on isotopy classes and respects disjoint unions of arcs; therefore it is a simplicial isomorphism. If S_τ has several components, an arc system is exactly a union of independent systems in the components, which is why the complex is the join of the component complexes. For connectivity, the Main Theorem implies each nonempty component arc complex is either contractible or a sphere; any such complex of positive dimension is connected, and joins are connected as soon as the total dimension is at least one. This link-connectivity is what allows a polygonal path between maximal simplices to be pushed off simplices of codimension greater than one.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 4.11] Let P be a finite bounded poset with minimum hat0 and maximum hat1. A rooted interval [u,v]_r means the interval [u,v] together with a fixed saturated chain r from hat0 to u. A chain-atom ordering assigns, for every rooted interval [u,hat1]_r, a total order on the atoms of that rooted interval, i.e. on the elements covering u; its restriction to [u,v]_r is the induced order on atoms lying below v. A recursive atom ordering (RAO), applied recursively to rooted intervals, is an atom order a1,...,at of a finite bounded rooted interval [u,v]_r such that: (R1a) for every j, [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j} has an RAO; (R1b) for j>1, in the chosen RAO of [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j}, all atoms x covering a_j that are above some earlier atom a_i with i<j precede all atoms not above any earlier atom; and (R2) for all i<j and all y with a_i<y and a_j<y, there exist k<j and z such that a_j ⋖ z, a_k<z, and z≤y. A generalized recursive atom ordering (GRAO) is defined the same way except that (R1b) is replaced by: (G1b) for every j and every x,w with a_j ⋖ x ⋖ w≤v, when the chosen GRAO of [a_j,v] is restricted to [a_j,w], either the first atom of [a_j,w] is above some earlier atom a_i with i<j, or no atom of [a_j,w] is above any earlier atom. Then P admits a GRAO if and only if P admits an RAO. More precisely, every RAO is a GRAO. Conversely, every GRAO can be transformed into an RAO by the atom reordering process: starting with a chain-atom ordering Λ, choose any linear extension of P; leave the atom order at hat0 unchanged; then, proceeding upward through the linear extension and through all roots r for [u,hat1], let u^- be the element immediately below u in r and r^- the root with u deleted. Using the already reordered atom order in [u^-,hat1]_{r^-}, put first all atoms x of [u,hat1]_r that are above some atom of [u^-,hat1]_{r^-} preceding u, in their original relative Λ-order, and then all remaining atoms, again in their original relative Λ-order. If Λ is a GRAO, the resulting chain-atom ordering Λ^re is an RAO.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: RAO ⇒ GRAO is by induction on longest chain length. Conditions (R1a) and (R2) are exactly the recursive and exchange conditions needed for GRAO. For (G1b), use that an RAO gives a CL-labeling and CL-labelings restrict to intervals; converting the restricted CL-labeling back to an RAO shows the RAO restricted to [a_j,w] still has the all earlier-overlap atoms first property, which implies the weaker GRAO alternative. For GRAO ⇒ RAO the proof builds a controlled reordering. Key preliminary facts: (1) a GRAO restricts to every rooted subinterval [u,v]_r; (2) the GRAO alternative extends from covers to arbitrary [a_j,v]: either the first atom is above an earlier atom or no atom is; (3) if two consecutive atoms a_i,a_{i+1} in [u,hat1]_r are never the first atom of any rooted interval [u,w]_r containing both, then swapping them preserves the GRAO. The swap lemma is the technical core: one proves the F and G sets of atoms above earlier atoms are unchanged by such a swap, then checks separately the recursive condition, the GRAO first-atom alternative, and the exchange condition. Next, the atom reordering process is shown to preserve the first atom of every rooted interval [u,v]_r. This is proved by induction on the root length. If the first atom x of [u,v]_r were moved behind another atom, then x lies in the G part. If x is above an earlier atom below u, the earliest atom in the lower interval [u^-,x] would have to move after u, contradicting induction. If no atom of [u,v]_r is above an earlier atom, then u was already first in [u^-,v] and remains first by induction, so every atom of [u,v]_r lies in G and x cannot move. Since the reordering preserves first atoms, each permutation it performs on atoms of [u,hat1]_r can be written as adjacent transpositions each preserving first atoms in all subintervals; the swap lemma implies the entire reordered chain-atom ordering remains a GRAO. Finally induct on longest chain length. Below each atom a_j, the reordered order is obtained from a GRAO on [a_j,hat1] by a first-atom-preserving initial permutation and then by atom reordering, hence is an RAO by induction. The construction itself enforces RAO condition (R1b), and because the output is still a GRAO it satisfies the common exchange condition (R2). Thus Λ^re is an RAO.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 6.4] Let P be a finite bounded poset. A chain-edge labeling labels each cover relation x⋖y with a value that may depend on the root, i.e. on the saturated chain from hat0 to x, with the usual consistency that identical initial rooted chains give identical labels on their common edges. For a rooted interval [u,v]_r, label sequences of saturated chains are compared lexicographically. A topological ascent at x⋖y⋖z means that the two-edge label sequence through y is lexicographically earliest among all saturated chains from x to z with the same root; otherwise it is a topological descent. A CL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique strictly ascending saturated chain and that chain has lexicographically first label sequence. A CC-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique saturated chain consisting entirely of topological ascents, and in each rooted interval all saturated chains have distinct label sequences with no label sequence a prefix of another. A TCL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique lexicographically earliest saturated chain and every other saturated chain has at least one topological descent. The unique earliest, or UE, property means that in every rooted interval [u,v]_r the smallest label occurring on an initial cover u⋖a, with a an atom of [u,v], occurs for exactly one such atom. A labeling with unique lexicographically earliest chains is self-consistent if whenever a is the atom on the lexicographically first saturated chain of [u,v]_r and b≠a is another atom of [u,v]_r, then for every v' with a,b≤v', every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through a is lexicographically earlier than every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through b. With RAO and GRAO as in Theorem 4.11, the following eight conditions on P are equivalent: (1) P admits an RAO; (2) P admits a GRAO; (3) P admits a CL-labeling; (4) P admits a CL-labeling with the UE property; (5) P admits a self-consistent CC-labeling; (6) P admits a CC-labeling with the UE property; (7) P admits a self-consistent TCL-labeling; (8) P admits a TCL-labeling with the UE property. Moreover, each implication is constructive: either the object satisfying one condition is already an object satisfying another, or the proof gives an explicit construction of the latter from the former.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: The proof is a web of constructive implications. (1)⇔(2) is Theorem 4.11: atom reordering turns any GRAO into an RAO, and every RAO is a GRAO. (1)⇔(3) is Björner-Wachs: finite bounded posets admitting RAOs are exactly CL-shellable posets. (3)⇔(4) uses Lemma 5.5, which proves every CL-labeling has UE. The key contradiction is: if two atoms a,a' of [u,v]_r share the minimum first label, take the lex-first chain M through a and the lex-first chain M' through a'. Since M is ascending, λ(u,a)< its next label; since M is lexicographically before M' and first labels agree, the next label on M is ≤ the next label on M'. Hence M' also ascends at a'. M' is ascending above a' because it is lex-first in [a',v], so [u,v]_r has two ascending chains, impossible. UE implies self-consistency (Lemma 5.3), because the unique atom with smallest first label beats every chain through every other atom in all larger intervals with the same root. RAOs also directly give CC-labelings with UE by labeling u⋖a_i with the index i in the recursively chosen atom order; distinct index sequences and the RAO exchange axiom force every non-first chain to have a topological descent. Conversely, a self-consistent TCL-labeling gives a GRAO (Theorem 6.3). Construct the atom order at [u,hat1]_r greedily: choose a1 from a lexicographically earliest saturated chain; after choosing a1,...,ai, choose a_{i+1} from a lexicographically earliest chain avoiding those atoms. Self-consistency makes this order compatible with lex-first choices in all subintervals. Inductively the upper intervals have GRAOs. For GRAO condition (G1b), suppose some atom of [a_j,w] is above an earlier atom but the first atom x1 of [a_j,w] is not. The lex-first chain through x1 then combines with the topological ascent hat0⋖a_j⋖x1 to give one topologically ascending chain to w, while the earlier atom below w gives another, contradicting uniqueness. For the exchange condition, if a_i<a_j both lie below y with i<j, the lex-first chain in [a_j,y] cannot remain topologically ascending from hat0, because the lex-first chain through the earlier atom already gives the unique topologically ascending chain; therefore there is a topological descent at the bottom, and the alternative two-step path supplies z and an earlier atom a_k<z with a_j⋖z≤y. CC-labelings are TCL-labelings because distinct non-prefix label sequences and uniqueness of the topologically ascending chain imply uniqueness of the lex-first chain and descents on all others. These implications give all eight equivalences, including the UE variants.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 7.1] For every n, let P_n be the uncrossing poset of complete matchings on 2n cyclically ordered boundary nodes, with an added minimum element hat0. The elements other than hat0 are perfect matchings, represented by strand diagrams. A noncrossing matching covers hat0. More generally, a matching u is covered by a matching v when v has rank one larger than u, where the rank is one plus the minimal possible number of strand crossings, and u is obtained from v by uncrossing a single crossing pair of strands in one of the two possible ways. The order is generated by these cover relations. Then P_n is dual CL-shellable; equivalently, the dual poset P_n^* admits a CL-labeling.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: Use the dual EC-labeling of Hersh-Kenyon. Encode a matching by numbering strands 1,...,n in the order their first endpoints are encountered clockwise from a fixed base boundary node, then reading the endpoint strand numbers clockwise; this gives a word of length 2n with two copies of each number and first occurrences increasing. If i<j and a crossing pair in v has subsequence i j i j, the two uncrossings replace it by either i j j i or i i j j. For a cover u⋖v in P_n, label the downward step v→u by (i,j) in the first case i j i j → i j j i, and by (j,i) in the second case i j i j → i i j j. Covers from noncrossing matchings down to hat0 receive a special label L. Order labels by (i,j)<L<(j,i) for i<j; among labels (i,j) with i<j use lexicographic order; among labels (j,i) with i<j use reverse lexicographic order. Hersh-Kenyon prove this is a dual EC-labeling of P_n. To upgrade to dual CL-shellability, apply the paper's Corollary 6.5, a consequence of Theorem 6.4: any self-consistent EC-labeling can be algorithmically converted to a CL-labeling. It remains to prove self-consistency, and the paper does so via the UE property. Fix an element v of P_n. The labels on all cover relations downward from v are distinct: a label K determines the two strand numbers being uncrossed, and its order, (i,j) versus (j,i), determines which of the two uncrossings was performed; this data uniquely determines the lower matching u. The special label L occurs only for the unique downward cover from a noncrossing matching to hat0. Hence, in the dual poset, all labels on covers upward from any fixed element are distinct, so in particular the smallest such label occurs uniquely. Thus the dual EC-labeling has the UE property. Lemma 5.3 gives UE ⇒ self-consistent, and Corollary 6.5 then yields a dual CL-labeling.

Let P_n be the uncrossing poset whose nonzero elements are pairings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points by n wires, with the fully crossed pairing as maximum and with an adjoined minimum 0-hat; a diagram D′ is covered by D in P_n when D′ is obtained from D by locally uncrossing one crossing pair of wires in one of the two possible ways and the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one, equivalently no pair of wires becomes double-crossed. If the cover uncrosses crossing wires k < m whose induced subword in w(D) is k,m,k,m, then the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,m,m,k has label (k,m), while the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,k,m,m has label (m,k). Covers from a noncrossing diagram to the artificial top of P_n^* are labeled L. Order labels by (i,j) with i < j lexicographically, then L, then labels (r,s) with r > s ordered by decreasing second coordinate and, for equal second coordinate, decreasing first coordinate: (n,n−1) < (n,n−2) < (n−1,n−2) < ... < (2,1).     Proof sketch: The proof is by analyzing intervals of P_n^*. If the start set changes, only labels (j,i) with j > i move p-endpoints. The imposed order on these labels forces the last such move: choose the smallest i whose v-end in the target either occupies a source start-position or contains nested below it a v-end occupying such a position; then the first coordinate j is determined by the word just before that position. If two different predecessors of v had the same forced label, the chain would require a lower label (t,i) and the local three-wire analysis of Proposition 4.3 would create a topological descent. A renaming argument shows this step cannot make a smaller label available, so the greedy label sequence is weakly increasing and ends with L. Finally, Lam's Eulerian theorem implies thinness, and the Björner-Danaraj-Klee criterion says a finite graded thin shellable poset with a minimum is a CW poset.

For i < j, define the noncrossing-pair set N(D) as follows: include (i,j) in N(D) if the induced subword of w(D) on wires i and j is i,j,j,i, and include (j,i) in N(D) if that induced subword is i,i,j,j. Thus neither ordered pair is in N(D) exactly when wires i and j cross, i.e. the induced subword is i,j,i,j. Consider a candidate downward cover D′ ≺ D in P_n obtained by uncrossing a crossing pair of wires in the way whose dual P_n^* edge-label would be the ordered pair (k,m), with k ≠ m. This candidate is an actual cover relation D′ ≺ D in P_n if and only if (k,m) is not in N(D), (m,k) is not in N(D), and the following additional conditions hold: if k < m, then for every l with k < l < m one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1; if k > m, then for every l with l < m or k < l one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1.  (from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: A downward step is a cover precisely when the chosen smoothing removes the crossing of wires k and m and does not introduce a double crossing; equivalently the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one. The ordered label (k,m) specifies which of the two smoothings is being tested. Pairs of wires not involving k or m are unchanged, and k,m themselves become noncrossing, so the only possible obstruction is a third wire l that becomes double-crossed with k or m. The cyclic order constraints from the endpoint naming greatly restrict which l can matter: for k < m only k < l < m can be dangerous; for k > m only l < m or k < l can be dangerous. For each such l one checks the allowable six-letter induced subwords on the three wires k,l,m. The smoothing labeled (k,m) is harmless exactly when, before smoothing, l is noncrossing in the specified orientation with exactly one of the two ordered pairs (k,l) and (l,m). If both ordered pairs are in N(D), or neither is, the local reconnection forces l to meet one of the other two wires twice, so the resulting diagram is not a cover. The two initial requirements (k,m),(m,k) not in N(D) say exactly that k and m really cross in D. This case check is the full no-double-crossing criterion, yielding the stated equivalence.

If they involve four distinct wires, the two uncrossings commute: performing them in the opposite order gives x ≺ y′ ≺ z. One then compares labels. Reversing the order preserves whether each individual label is of increasing type (a,b) with a < b or decreasing type (a,b) with a > b, because deleting unrelated wires does not change which endpoint of the two-wire pair is first. The paper checks the resulting cases from the label order: if both labels are decreasing, a descent forces the second coordinate of the first label to be smaller, making the swapped first label smaller; if the first is decreasing and the second increasing, either the increasing label is automatically smaller or its first coordinate is smaller; if both are increasing, a descent forces the second uncrossing's first coordinate to be smaller. In each case λ(x,y′) < λ(x,y). If the two uncrossings involve only three wires, restrict the diagram to those three wires, giving the corresponding rank-two situation in P_3^*. The relative order of relevant labels is preserved under this restriction and under reinclusion: increasing versus decreasing type is preserved, comparisons of minima are preserved, and ties are broken by the same comparison of maxima. A direct check of the labeled Hasse diagram of P_3^* shows every descent there has a lexicographically earlier alternate middle element. Lifting that alternate chain back to the original three wires inside P_n^* gives the required y′. The hypothesis z below the artificial top avoids the special L-labeled final cover, so all cases are covered.

For a rooted plane tree with n edges, label its half-edges 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise around the tree from the root; write e(u,v) for the edge whose two half-edges have labels u and v. A plane tree is P-valid if for every edge e(u,v), the letters p_u and p_v are complementary. Fix indices i<j<i'<j'. The local moves are as follows: a type 1 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') occur, replaces them by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type 2 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') occur, replaces them by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move on a P-valid tree is valid if the resulting tree is also P-valid. Then: (1) there is a valid type 1 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). (2) there is a valid type 2 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B).  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: The combinatorial hypotheses, namely that the two relevant edges are adjacent and have indices i<j<i'<j', ensure that the stated replacement is a legal local move of plane trees. Validity is then exactly a four-letter condition. For type 1, the starting tree is P-valid, so p_i is complementary to p_j and p_{i'} is complementary to p_{j'}. The resulting tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_{j'} and p_j is complementary to p_{i'}. Let B=p_i. Since complements are unique and comp is an involution with no fixed points, the four complementary-pair requirements force p_j=comp(B), p_{j'}=comp(B), and p_{i'}=B. Conversely, if these equalities hold, then both original edges e(i,j), e(i',j') and both new edges e(i,j'), e(j,i') pair complementary letters, so the type 1 move is valid. The type 2 statement is the same argument with the initial and final pairs interchanged: initial validity of e(i,j') and e(j,i') and final validity of e(i,j), e(i',j') impose precisely the same equalities p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). The proposition is therefore an exact local label criterion for validity of the elementary move.

At step j, if the stack is nonempty and its top entry is an indexed letter (i,p_i) with p_i=comp(p_j), pop that top entry and record the ordered pair (i,j). Otherwise push (j,p_j). Let E(P) be the set of pairs recorded by the algorithm. Let V(P) denote the set of P-valid plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of {1,...,2n} in which matched positions carry complementary letters. If V(P) is nonempty, then the greedy algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and the recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T_0(P) in V(P).  (from: literature_1501.03238)

If the algorithm pops p_i at step j, then while p_i sat on the stack all letters p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that were pushed above it must have been popped before step j. Hence the algorithm matches the whole interval {i,i+1,...,j} internally by a perfect matching, and deleting the contiguous block p_i...p_j from P leaves exactly the same stack evolution and recorded pairs outside the block. This is the LIFO/nesting mechanism that makes the greedy pairs noncrossing. To prove the theorem, assume for contradiction that Q=q_1...q_{2k} is a shortest word admitting at least one Q-valid plane tree but for which the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. A valid plane tree has a leaf edge e(s,s+1), so q_s and q_{s+1} are complementary; therefore the greedy algorithm must pop at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1; since there were no earlier pops, this pop removes q_j. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} leaves a shorter word on which the greedy algorithm has the same final stack as it has on Q. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), removing that leaf gives a valid plane tree for the shorter word, contradicting minimality of Q. It remains only to ensure the existence of such a valid tree. Take any Q-valid tree. If it does not already contain e(j,j+1), the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 occur in one of the few possible noncrossing configurations: both on left sides of adjacent edges, both on right sides of adjacent edges, or forming the peak configuration displayed in the paper. Since q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, their partners have the complementary labels needed for Proposition 2.7, so an appropriate valid local move transforms the tree into one containing the leaf e(j,j+1). Deleting that leaf gives the desired shorter valid instance, contradiction. Therefore no minimal counterexample exists; whenever a valid plane tree exists, the greedy stack empties and its nested complementary pairs define a P-valid plane tree.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.6 and Corollaries 3.8, 3.10] Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p_1...p_{2n} be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T_0(P) be the P-valid plane tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose undirected edges join two trees related by a valid local move. Let G_P^+ be the directed graph with the same vertices, with a directed edge T -> T' exactly when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T_0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+. Equivalently, T_0(P) has no outgoing valid type 2 move, and every P-valid plane tree T different from T_0(P) has at least one outgoing valid type 2 move. Moreover G_P^+ has no directed cycles, and from every P-valid plane tree T there is a directed path in G_P^+ consisting entirely of valid type 2 moves from T to T_0(P). If T_0 contained adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' supporting a valid type 2 move, Proposition 2.7 would give p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). In the greedy construction of T_0, by the subtree/nesting property of plane trees, all material between i and j has already been matched internally when step j is reached, so p_i is on top of the stack. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pair i with j, producing e(i,j), contradiction. Next prove every T != T_0 has a valid type 2 move. Let j be the first half-edge at which T and T_0 differ in left/right status. If j were a left half-edge in T_0 and a right half-edge in T, say in edge e(i',j), then the interval i'+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T; all its earlier half-edge statuses agree with T_0, hence the same nested matching occurs in T_0. Thus p_{i'} is on top of the greedy stack at step j and would be paired with p_j, forcing e(i',j) in T_0, contradiction. Therefore j is a right half-edge in T_0, paired there with some i<j, while i and j are both left half-edges in T. The interval i+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T_0 and, by minimality of j, the same subtree occurs in T. Thus in T the edges beginning at i and j are adjacent in precisely the configuration of a type 2 move. Since T_0 pairs i with j, p_i and p_j are complementary; P-validity of T gives complementary partners on the two adjacent edges, and Proposition 2.7 makes the type 2 move valid. This proves uniqueness of the sink. For acyclicity, use the height function d_T=sum_v dist(v,v_0), where v_0 is the root. In the schematic for a type 2 move, the move decreases this total distance by at least one, and by more if the intervening subtree denoted C in the paper is nonempty; no type 2 move can preserve or increase d_T. Hence G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Since the graph is finite and every non-T_0 vertex has an outgoing edge, following any sequence of outgoing type 2 moves must eventually terminate, and the only possible terminal vertex is the unique sink T_0. Thus every valid plane tree has a directed type 2 path to T_0. The paper also proves connectedness of G_P independently by induction: two valid trees either share an edge, allowing induction on the inside and outside subtrees, or a valid local move can first be applied to one tree to create a shared edge.
