## Verdict

All four audited points are true under the literal admissibility definition. The only caveat is terminological: the six-endpoint object \(a,A,a,A,a,A\) should be treated as an **auxiliary labelled skeleton complex**, not as an instance satisfying the original “quasi-reduced” hypothesis, since it contains \(aAa\). The padded word is quasi-reduced, and the deletion map gives the needed cellular identification with that auxiliary skeleton.

---

## Lemma 1 — Forest, no repeated crossings, and endpoint-interleaving parity

Let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the planar graph obtained from \(M\) by making boundary endpoints and crossings vertices.

**Claim.** If \(M\) is admissible, then:

1. \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.
2. No two intervals cross more than once.
3. For two intervals with endpoints \(i<j<k<\ell\), they cross iff the pairing is \((i,k),(j,\ell)\). Thus crossing status is forced by endpoint interleaving.

**Proof.** If \(\Gamma(M)\) had a graph cycle, choose a simple cycle. Boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), so the cycle lies in the open half-plane. It bounds a disk. Some complementary region inside has an \(M\)-boundary component with no real-line boundary endpoints, contradicting the admissibility condition that every such component have two boundary endpoints labelled by inverse letters.

If two intervals met twice, the subarc of one between two consecutive common points and the corresponding subarc of the other form a simple closed curve in \(\Gamma(M)\), again contradicting the forest property.

Finally, compactify the half-plane to a disk. A properly embedded arc separates the disk into two components. A second arc crosses it mod \(2\) iff its endpoints lie on opposite boundary arcs, i.e. iff the two endpoint pairs interleave. Since repeated crossings are impossible, the mod-\(2\) crossing number \(0\) or \(1\) is the actual crossing number. ∎

---

## Lemma 2 — The named skeleton cells are unique isotopy classes

In the skeleton with labels

\[
1:a,\quad 2:A,\quad 3:a,\quad 4:A,\quad 5:a,\quad 6:A,
\]

define

\[
\begin{aligned}
Q_1&=(13)(25)(46),&
Q_2&=(14)(26)(35),&
Q_3&=(15)(24)(36),
\end{aligned}
\]

and

\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]

**Claim.** Each listed endpoint pairing determines a single admissible isotopy class of embedded matchings. No extra isotopy classes arise from wiggly or nonstandard arcs.

**Proof.** By Lemma 1, the crossing pairs are exactly those visible from endpoint interleaving. Thus the only possible ambiguity would be the order of crossings along an interval or the placement of a disjoint interval.

For an arc \((p,q)\), let \(U_{p,q}\) be the side incident to the boundary interval \((p,q)\). Jordan separation implies:

- an arc with both endpoints in \((p,q)\) and disjoint from \((p,q)\) lies in \(U_{p,q}\);
- an arc with both endpoints outside \((p,q)\) lies on the other side;
- an arc with one endpoint inside and one outside crosses \((p,q)\) exactly once.

This forces the crossing orders in the three \(Q_i\):

- In \(Q_1=(13)(25)(46)\), the central arc \((25)\) starts inside the side of \((13)\) and ends inside the side of \((46)\), while \((13)\) and \((46)\) are separated. Hence \((25)\) crosses \((13)\) first and \((46)\) second.
- In \(Q_2=(14)(26)(35)\), the arc \((35)\) lies inside the side of \((26)\). Thus \((14)\), going from outside \((26)\) to inside \((35)\), crosses \((26)\) first and \((35)\) second.
- In \(Q_3=(15)(24)(36)\), the arc \((24)\) lies inside the side of \((15)\). Thus \((36)\), going from inside \((24)\) to outside \((15)\), crosses \((24)\) first and \((15)\) second.

For the \(P_i\), there is only one crossing pair, and the remaining disjoint interval is forced into the unique side determined by its boundary endpoints. Hence each \(P_i\) also gives one planar forest embedding.

A standard planar-graph isotopy fact now applies: two embeddings of the same finite plane forest in a disk, with the same boundary vertices and the same cyclic orders at vertices, are ambient isotopic rel boundary. Therefore the named pairings give exactly the intended cells.

Admissibility follows because in each component the cyclically consecutive leaves have alternating labels \(a,A,a,A,\dots\), hence inverse labels. ∎

---

## Lemma 3 — The smoothing facets are exactly the stated \(P_i\)

For a crossing between \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<j<k<\ell\), the two local smoothings produce

\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]

Using Lemma 2 to identify each resulting endpoint pairing with its unique one-cell, the facets are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{2-cell} & \text{crossing resolved} & \text{two facets}\\
\hline
Q_1=(13)(25)(46) & (13)\times(25) & P_1,\ P_2\\
Q_1=(13)(25)(46) & (25)\times(46) & P_3,\ P_4\\
Q_2=(14)(26)(35) & (14)\times(26) & P_1,\ P_5\\
Q_2=(14)(26)(35) & (14)\times(35) & P_4,\ P_6\\
Q_3=(15)(24)(36) & (15)\times(36) & P_3,\ P_5\\
Q_3=(15)(24)(36) & (24)\times(36) & P_2,\ P_6.
\end{array}
\]

Therefore over \(\mathbb F_2\),

\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]

\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]

\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]

So

\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0.
\]

---

## Lemma 4 — Forced adjacent caps and cellular deletion

For the padded word

\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]

the five filler pairs are

\[
(2,3),\quad (5,6),\quad (8,9),\quad (11,12),\quad (14,15).
\]

**Claim.** In every admissible matching, these are isolated uncrossed adjacent caps, and deleting them gives a cellular isomorphism with the six-endpoint skeleton.

**Proof.** Use the side-neighbor consequence of the forest lemma: if a tree component has boundary leaves \(x_1<\cdots<x_m\), then cyclically consecutive leaves must have inverse labels. If a letter \(x\) and its inverse \(X\) each occur only once, then the two side-neighbors of the \(x\)-leaf must both be the unique \(X\)-leaf. Hence the component has only two leaves.

For a tree component with \(m\) leaves and \(v\) crossing vertices,

\[
m+4v=2(m+v-1),
\]

so

\[
m=2v+2.
\]

Thus \(m=2\) implies \(v=0\). Therefore each unique filler pair is joined by a single uncrossed interval.

Since each such pair is adjacent on the boundary, the interval and the boundary segment between its endpoints bound a disk containing no marked boundary point. No other interval can enter that disk without crossing the cap. Hence the cap is boundary-parallel and can be isotoped to a fixed standard small cap.

Deleting the five standard caps and relabelling the remaining endpoints

\[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16
\]

as

\[
1,\dots,6
\]

produces the skeleton labelled

\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

Conversely, any skeleton matching can be lifted uniquely by reinserting the five standard caps. Caps have no crossings, so crossing sets are unchanged. Resolving a skeleton crossing before or after inserting caps gives the same result. Therefore deletion and reinsertion identify cells and attaching maps. ∎

---

## Lemma 5 — No hidden \(3\)-cells or duplicate \(2\)-cells

In the six-endpoint skeleton, Lemma 1 implies that the crossing number of a pairing is exactly the number of interleaving endpoint pairs.

Among the \(15\) endpoint pairings:

- the only two-crossing pairings are \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\);
- the only one-crossing pairings are \(P_1,\dots,P_6\);
- the unique three-crossing pairing is \((14)(25)(36)\).

But \((14)(25)(36)\) has all three intervals pairwise crossing. Its subdivided graph contains a cycle, so it is inadmissible by the forest lemma. Therefore the skeleton has no admissible \(3\)-cells.

By Lemma 2, the \(Q_i\) do not split into multiple isotopy classes. Hence there are no duplicate \(2\)-cells arising from nonstandard embeddings. By Lemma 4, the same holds in the padded word complex.

Thus the arbitrary-embedded-interval issue does not introduce hidden cells, duplicate cells, or altered facets.

The counterexample proof therefore survives the audit: the lifted chain \(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\) is a nonzero mod-\(2\) \(2\)-cycle, and since there are no \(3\)-cells, it is not a boundary.

## Referee verdict

I find **no genuine gap** in the proposed counterexample, provided the final write-up makes the planar-subset convention at crossings explicit and treats \(a,A,a,A,a,A\) only as an auxiliary six-endpoint skeleton.

The counterexample word is

\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]

with uppercase letters denoting inverses. It freely reduces to \(1\), and direct inspection shows that it contains no consecutive triple \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\). The proof should then proceed through the following lemmas.

---

## Lemmas that should appear in the final proof

### Lemma 1: Literal side-arcs and the forest lemma

Let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the planar graph obtained from \(M\) by declaring marked endpoints and crossings to be vertices. For a complementary region \(R\), a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is a literal side-component of this planar subset: at a crossing it follows the two adjacent half-edges bounding the sector of \(R\). If such a component closes up, branches, or has a crossing as an endpoint, it does **not** have boundary consisting of two marked boundary points, so admissibility fails.

**Statement.** If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest. Consequently, no two intervals cross more than once, and crossing between two intervals is forced by endpoint interleaving.

**Reason.** A graph cycle lies in the open half-plane and bounds a Jordan disk containing no marked endpoints. Some complementary region inside that disk has an \(M\)-boundary component with no marked boundary endpoints, contradicting admissibility. Repeated crossings give a bigon cycle. Endpoint interleaving gives odd mod-\(2\) intersection number, and non-interleaving gives even mod-\(2\) intersection number; since repeated crossings are impossible, this determines actual crossing.

---

### Lemma 2: Side-neighbor lemma

**Statement.** Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\), with boundary leaves \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) in cyclic boundary order. For each cyclically consecutive pair \(x_i,x_{i+1}\), the unique path in \(T\) between them occurs as a side-component of some complementary region. Hence admissibility forces

\[
w_{x_i}=w_{x_{i+1}}^{-1}.
\]

Conversely, for a planar forest, all side-components arise this way.

**Reason.** For \(T\) alone, this is the standard regular-neighborhood/fatgraph description of a plane tree: each side of the tree runs along the path between consecutive leaves. Other matching components do not invalidate this: a sufficiently small one-sided collar of that side path avoids all other components, so the same path remains an \(M\)-boundary component in the full complement.

This lemma is the right repair for the most delicate point.

---

### Lemma 3: Unique inverse pairs are forced uncrossed intervals

**Statement.** If a letter \(x\) and its inverse \(X\) occur exactly once each in \(w\), then in every admissible matching the \(x\)-endpoint is paired directly with the \(X\)-endpoint by an uncrossed isolated interval.

**Reason.** In the tree component containing the \(x\)-leaf, both cyclic side-neighbors of that leaf must be labelled \(X\). Since there is only one \(X\)-leaf, the component has exactly two leaves. If a tree component has \(m\) leaves and \(v\) crossing vertices, then

\[
m+4v=2(m+v-1),
\]

so \(m=2v+2\). Thus \(m=2\) implies \(v=0\).

Applied to \(w\), the filler pairs

\[
(2,3),\ (5,6),\ (8,9),\ (11,12),\ (14,15)
\]

are forced isolated uncrossed adjacent caps.

---

### Lemma 4: Deleting the caps is a cellular identification

Because the five forced intervals are adjacent caps, each bounds an empty boundary disk and is isotopic rel boundary to a standard small cap. Deleting them leaves the six endpoints

\[
1,4,7,10,13,16
\]

with labels

\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

After relabelling these as \(1,\dots,6\), one obtains an auxiliary six-endpoint skeleton complex \(K\). Deleting and reinserting the caps gives a **cellular isomorphism** between \(F_w\) and \(K\): crossings are unchanged, and resolving a skeleton crossing commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps.

This should be stated as a cellular identification, not merely as a homotopy equivalence.

---

### Lemma 5: Six-endpoint skeleton enumeration and uniqueness

In the skeleton labelled

\[
1:a,\quad 2:A,\quad 3:a,\quad 4:A,\quad 5:a,\quad 6:A,
\]

the admissible \(2\)-cells are exactly

\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),\quad
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),\quad
Q_3=(15)(24)(36),
\]

and the admissible \(1\)-cells are exactly

\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]

Each listed endpoint pairing determines a unique isotopy class. The final write-up should explicitly justify this: endpoint interleaving fixes which pairs cross, forestness forbids repeated crossings, and Jordan separation forces the order of the two crossings along the central interval in each \(Q_i\).

There are no hidden \(3\)-cells. One clean proof is the forest degree count: for a forest with \(m=6\) leaves, \(v\) crossing vertices, and \(c\ge 1\) components,

\[
6=2v+2c,
\]

so \(v=3-c\le 2\). Thus no admissible skeleton matching can have three crossings.

---

## Homology calculation

Over \(\mathbb F_2\), the relevant cellular boundaries are

\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]

\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]

\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]

Hence every \(P_i\) appears twice in

\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3),
\]

so

\[
z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3
\]

is a nonzero mod-\(2\) \(2\)-cycle. Since there are no \(3\)-cells, \(z\) is not a boundary. Thus

\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0,
\]

so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

---

## Wording pitfalls to avoid

1. Do **not** call \(a,A,a,A,a,A\) a quasi-reduced word complex. It is only an auxiliary skeleton.
2. Do **not** split strands abstractly at crossings. Use the literal planar subset \(\Gamma(M)\).
3. Do **not** leave cap deletion as a homotopy argument; it is a cell-preserving identification.
4. Do **not** ignore possible duplicate isotopy classes; explicitly cite plane-forest uniqueness.
5. Do **not** count \((14)(25)(36)\) as a \(3\)-cell; it contains a cycle and is inadmissible.

With those clarifications, I would certify the counterexample.

w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
For a word \(u=u_1\cdots u_{2n}\), let \(F_u\) be the CW complex whose \(k\)-cells are isotopy classes of admissible \(k\)-crossing matchings: collections of \(n\) properly embedded intervals in \(H=\mathbb R\times[0,\infty)\) with boundary \(\{1,\dots,2n\}\times\{0\}\), only transverse pairwise intersections and no triple points, such that for every component \(R\) of \(H\setminus M\) and every component \(Y\) of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\), the boundary of \(Y\) consists of two real-line endpoints \((i,0),(j,0)\) with \(u_i=u_j^{-1}\). The \(k\)-cell associated to \(M\) is the \(k\)-cube whose coordinates are the crossings of \(M\), and its facets attach by resolving one crossing.

The word \(w\) above is reducible and quasi-reduced, and
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\ne 0.
\]
Hence \(F_w\) is not contractible. This is not a relaxation of the original question; it gives a concrete counterexample to contractibility in general.

## Proof

### 1. The word

The word is
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
In \(G\),
\[
w=a(bb^{-1})a^{-1}(cc^{-1})a(dd^{-1})a^{-1}(ee^{-1})a(ff^{-1})a^{-1}=1,
\]
so \(w\) is reducible.

It is quasi-reduced. Indeed, the only adjacent inverse pairs of the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\) are
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]
The following letters are respectively
\[
A,\quad a,\quad A,\quad a,\quad A,
\]
none of which equals \(b,c,d,e,f\), respectively. No other adjacent pair is of the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\). Thus no subword \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\) occurs.

### 2. The graph \(\Gamma(M)\) and the forest lemma

Given a matching \(M\), define \(\Gamma(M)\) to be the finite planar graph obtained from \(M\) by declaring every crossing and every real-line endpoint to be a vertex. The edges of \(\Gamma(M)\) are the open arc-segments between consecutive vertices along the original intervals.

We use the admissibility condition literally: if, for some complementary region \(R\), a component \(Y\) of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is not an arc whose boundary consists of two real-line endpoints with inverse labels, then \(M\) is not admissible.

**Forest lemma.** If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.

Suppose \(\Gamma(M)\) contains a graph cycle \(C\). Boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), so \(C\) uses no boundary endpoint. Hence \(C\subset\mathbb R\times(0,\infty)\). As an embedded graph cycle, \(C\) is a simple closed curve. By the Jordan curve theorem, it bounds a disk \(D\), and since the real line is disjoint from \(C\) and lies in the unbounded component, \(D\) is contained in the open upper half-plane.

The finite graph \(M\cap D\) does not fill \(D\), so \(D\setminus M\) has a complementary component \(U\). Because \(C\subset M\), this component cannot connect to the outside of \(D\) without crossing \(M\). Thus \(U\) is contained in a component \(R\) of \(H\setminus M\) with \(\overline R\subset D\). Therefore every component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) lies away from the real line. Its boundary cannot consist of two points \((i,0),(j,0)\). This contradicts admissibility. Hence \(\Gamma(M)\) has no graph cycle.

### 3. Side-neighbor lemma

Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\), and let its boundary leaves be
\[
x_1<x_2<\cdots<x_m
\]
along the real line. We regard these cyclically, so \(x_m\) and \(x_1\) are consecutive through infinity.

Compactify \(H\) to a closed disk. Let \(J_i\) be the boundary arc from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\), with indices mod \(m\). First ignore all components of \(M\) other than \(T\). Consider the planar graph \(K=T\cup\partial D\). If \(T\) has \(v\) vertices, then \(T\) has \(v-1\) edges, and \(\partial D\) is cut into \(m\) boundary edges. Thus
\[
E(K)=v-1+m,\qquad V(K)=v.
\]
Euler’s formula gives
\[
F(K)=E(K)-V(K)+2=m+1.
\]
One face is outside the disk, so \(D\setminus T\) has \(m\) components.

No component of \(D\setminus T\) can avoid \(\partial D\); otherwise its boundary would be contained entirely in \(T\), forcing a cycle in the tree \(T\). Since there are \(m\) boundary arcs \(J_i\), each component of \(D\setminus T\) is incident to exactly one \(J_i\). The component incident to \(J_i\) has \(T\)-boundary equal to the unique path in \(T\) from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\). Extra branches would lead to another boundary leaf and hence to another boundary arc, impossible.

Restoring the other components of \(M\) does not change this local conclusion: a small one-sided collar of such a path in \(T\) avoids all other components. Therefore the side paths of \(T\) connect cyclically consecutive leaves. By admissibility, the labels on every cyclically consecutive pair of leaves of \(T\) must be inverse.

### 4. Unique inverse letters force isolated uncrossed intervals

Suppose a letter \(x\) occurs exactly once in a word, at endpoint \(p\), and \(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once, at endpoint \(q\). Let \(T\) be the component of \(\Gamma(M)\) containing \(p\). The two cyclic neighbors of \(p\) among the leaves of \(T\) must both have label \(x^{-1}\), by the side-neighbor lemma. Since \(q\) is the only such endpoint, both neighbors are \(q\). Thus \(T\) has exactly two leaves.

If \(T\) has \(m\) boundary leaves and \(v\) crossing vertices, then all crossing vertices have degree \(4\), all leaves have degree \(1\), and, since \(T\) is a tree,
\[
E(T)=m+v-1.
\]
Hence
\[
m+4v=2E(T)=2(m+v-1),
\]
so
\[
m=2v+2.
\]
With \(m=2\), we get \(v=0\). Thus \(T\) is a single uncrossed interval joining \(p\) to \(q\), isolated from the rest of the matching.

In our word, the letters \(b,c,d,e,f\) and their inverses occur exactly once. Hence every admissible matching contains the five isolated uncrossed intervals
\[
(2,3),\quad (5,6),\quad (8,9),\quad (11,12),\quad (14,15).
\]

Because each pair is adjacent on the real line, each forced interval and the boundary segment between its endpoints bound an empty disk: no other interval can enter that disk without crossing the cap or having an endpoint in the open boundary segment. Therefore these intervals are boundary-parallel caps.

Deleting these five caps and compressing the gaps gives a cell-preserving correspondence with the six remaining endpoints
\[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16,
\]
which we relabel as \(1,\dots,6\). Their labels are
\[
a,\ A,\ a,\ A,\ a,\ A.
\]
The deleted caps have no crossings, adding or deleting them preserves admissibility of all remaining side components, and resolving a crossing among the remaining intervals commutes with adding or deleting the caps. Hence it suffices to work in this six-endpoint skeleton and then reinsert the forced caps.

### 5. Three admissible \(2\)-cells in the skeleton

In the skeleton, write a pairing as \((ij)(kl)(mn)\). For standard chord diagrams, two chords \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\) with
\[
i<j<k<\ell
\]
cross once. Nested pairs, for example \((15)\) and \((24)\), do not cross.

Define
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),\qquad
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),\qquad
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]
Each \(Q_i\) has exactly two crossings:

\[
Q_1:\ (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46);
\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35);
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]
For \(Q_3\), the pair \((15),(24)\) is nested, since \(1<2<4<5\), so it does not cross.

In each case the crossing graph is a path on the three intervals, so \(\Gamma(Q_i)\) is a connected tree with six boundary leaves. The cyclic leaf labels are
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A,
\]
so consecutive cyclic leaves have inverse labels. By the side-neighbor lemma, each \(Q_i\) is admissible. Thus \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) give \(2\)-cells.

Now define six \(1\)-cells:
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]
Each \(P_i\) is admissible: it consists of one four-leaf crossing component with alternating labels, together with one isolated inverse-labelled interval.

### 6. Boundary computation over \(\mathbb F_2\)

At a crossing of chords \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\) with \(i<j<k<\ell\), the two resolutions are
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k),
\]
leaving all other intervals unchanged.

Over \(\mathbb F_2\), signs do not matter. Resolving the crossings of \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) gives:
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)
&=(P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4)\\
&\quad +(P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6)\\
&\quad +(P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6)\\
&=0
\end{aligned}
\]
in \(C_1(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\), since each \(P_i\) appears twice.

Thus
\[
z:=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3
\]
is a nonzero \(2\)-cycle after reinserting the five forced caps into the original word complex.

### 7. No \(3\)-cells

After deleting the forced caps, only \(m=3\) intervals remain. Let an admissible skeleton matching have \(k\) crossings and \(c\) connected components in its graph \(\Gamma\). By the forest lemma, \(\Gamma\) is a forest.

Counting vertices and edges:
\[
V=2m+k,
\]
because there are \(2m\) boundary endpoints and \(k\) crossing vertices, while
\[
E=m+2k,
\]
because each interval is cut into one more edge than the number of crossings on it, and each crossing lies on two intervals.

For a forest with \(c\) components,
\[
E=V-c.
\]
Hence
\[
m+2k=2m+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=m-c\le m-1=2.
\]
Thus no admissible skeleton matching has \(3\) or more crossings. Since the forced caps never carry crossings, the original complex \(F_w\) has no \(3\)-cells. Therefore
\[
C_3(F_w;\mathbb F_2)=0.
\]

The nonzero \(2\)-cycle \(z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\) cannot be a boundary, because there are no \(3\)-chains. Hence
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\ne 0.
\]
A contractible space has trivial second homology, so \(F_w\) is not contractible. This proves that \(F_w\) need not be contractible.

response:
**Claim.** Let \(G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle\), write
\[
A=a^{-1},\quad B=b^{-1},\quad C=c^{-1},\quad D=d^{-1},\quad E=e^{-1},\quad F=f^{-1},
\]
and set
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
Then \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced, but the associated CW complex \(F_w\) satisfies
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]
In particular, \(F_w\) is not contractible.

## 1. Two planar facts about admissible matchings

Let \(H=\mathbb R\times[0,\infty)\). For a matching \(M\), let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the finite graph obtained by declaring the boundary endpoints and all crossings to be vertices, and by taking as edges the arc-segments between consecutive vertices along the matching intervals.

A matching will be called admissible if it satisfies the complementary-region inverse-label condition from the definition of \(F_w\).

### Lemma 1: admissible graphs are forests

If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.

Indeed, suppose \(\Gamma(M)\) contained a graph cycle \(\gamma\). Since boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), \(\gamma\) lies in the interior of \(H\). As an embedded simple closed curve, \(\gamma\) separates the plane. Let \(D_\gamma\) be its bounded complementary disk. The real line lies outside \(D_\gamma\).

Choose a component \(R\) of \(H\setminus M\) contained in \(D_\gamma\). Such a component exists because \(M\) is a finite graph. Since \(\gamma\subset M\), \(R\) cannot connect to the outside of \(D_\gamma\). Hence every component of \(M\cap \partial\overline R\) lies away from the real line, so its boundary cannot consist of two marked boundary points \((i,0),(j,0)\). That contradicts admissibility. Therefore \(\Gamma(M)\) has no cycles.

### Lemma 2: side-path criterion

Compactify \(H\) by adding the point at infinity, obtaining a closed disk \(\overline H\) whose boundary circle contains the marked points in cyclic order.

Let \(N\) be a matching such that \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest. Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(N)\), and let its boundary leaves be
\[
x_1,\dots,x_m
\]
in cyclic order on \(\partial \overline H\). Let \(P_i\subset T\) be the unique tree path from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\), indices modulo \(m\).

Then the components of \(N\cap \partial\overline R\) lying in \(T\), as \(R\) ranges over components of \(\overline H\setminus N\), are precisely the paths \(P_i\). Consequently, \(N\) is admissible if and only if, for every tree component \(T\), every cyclically consecutive pair of leaves of \(T\) has inverse labels.

Proof. First consider the tree \(T\) alone. Let \(J_i\) be the boundary arc from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\). For the planar graph
\[
K=T\cup \partial\overline H,
\]
if \(T\) has \(v\) vertices, then \(K\) has \(v\) vertices and \((v-1)+m\) edges. Euler’s formula gives \(m+1\) faces, one outside \(\overline H\), so \(\overline H\setminus T\) has \(m\) components.

No component of \(\overline H\setminus T\) can avoid \(\partial\overline H\), because then its boundary would be a closed walk in the tree \(T\), forcing a cycle. Hence each of the \(m\) components is incident to exactly one boundary arc \(J_i\). The component incident to \(J_i\) has \(T\)-boundary equal to the unique path \(P_i\) from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\).

Now restore the other forest components of \(N\). They are compact and disjoint from \(T\), so a sufficiently thin one-sided collar of each path \(P_i\) is disjoint from them. Thus the full complementary regions of \(\overline H\setminus N\) adjacent to \(T\) still have \(T\)-boundary exactly one of the paths \(P_i\). Therefore the only possible components of \(N\cap \partial\overline R\) lying in \(T\) are those side paths.

Thus the admissibility condition is exactly that the endpoints of every such path \(P_i\) have inverse labels.

### Lemma 3: unique inverse pairs force uncrossed caps

Suppose a label \(x\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(p\), and \(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(q\). In any admissible matching, \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by a single uncrossed interval.

Let \(T\) be the tree component containing \(p\). By the side-path criterion, both cyclic neighbors of \(p\) among the leaves of \(T\) must have label \(x^{-1}\). Since \(q\) is the only such leaf, both neighbors are \(q\). Hence \(T\) has exactly two leaves.

If \(T\) has \(m\) leaves and \(s\) crossing vertices, then all leaves have degree \(1\), all crossings have degree \(4\), and \(T\) is a tree. Thus
\[
m+4s=2E(T)=2(m+s-1),
\]
so
\[
m=2s+2.
\]
With \(m=2\), we get \(s=0\). Therefore \(T\) is a single uncrossed interval joining \(p\) to \(q\).

## 2. The word \(w\)

We have
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
In the free group,
\[
w=a(bb^{-1})a^{-1}(cc^{-1})a(dd^{-1})a^{-1}(ee^{-1})a(ff^{-1})a^{-1}=1,
\]
so \(w\) is reducible.

It is quasi-reduced. The only adjacent inverse pairs are
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF,
\]
and the following letters are respectively
\[
A,\quad a,\quad A,\quad a,\quad A,
\]
none of which repeats the first letter of the inverse pair. Hence there is no subword \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\).

By Lemma 3, every admissible matching for \(w\) contains the five forced uncrossed intervals
\[
(2,3),\quad (5,6),\quad (8,9),\quad (11,12),\quad (14,15),
\]
joining
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]

Each such interval has adjacent boundary endpoints. Since it is uncrossed, it together with the boundary segment between its endpoints bounds a disk containing no marked boundary point. No other interval can enter that disk without crossing the forced interval or having an endpoint on that boundary segment. Hence each forced interval is a unique boundary-parallel cap up to isotopy rel endpoints.

Deleting those five caps and reindexing the remaining boundary points gives a cellular bijection with the auxiliary six-point word
\[
u=a\,A\,a\,A\,a\,A.
\]
The remaining original positions
\[
1,4,7,10,13,16
\]
become skeleton positions
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6.
\]

Admissibility is preserved in both directions by the side-path criterion: non-cap tree components have the same cyclic leaf orders and labels after deletion, while each inserted cap is a two-leaf inverse-labelled tree. Crossings are unchanged, and resolving a crossing commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps. Thus \(F_w\) is cellularly isomorphic to the corresponding skeleton complex for \(u=aAaAaA\).

## 3. Three admissible \(2\)-cells in the skeleton

Work now with the six labelled boundary points
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6
\]
with labels
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

Write \((ij)(kl)(mn)\) for the standard chord diagram joining \(i\) to \(j\), \(k\) to \(l\), and \(m\) to \(n\), with two chords crossing exactly once when their endpoints interleave.

Consider the three \(2\)-crossing matchings
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),
\]
\[
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),
\]
\[
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]
Their crossings are:
\[
Q_1:\ (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46);
\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35);
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]
In each case the graph is a connected tree with six boundary leaves in cyclic order
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6,
\]
whose labels are
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]
Thus every cyclically consecutive pair has inverse labels, so by the side-path criterion each \(Q_i\) is admissible. Hence each \(Q_i\) determines a \(2\)-cell.

Now define six admissible \(1\)-cells:
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]
Each \(P_i\) consists of one four-leaf crossing tree with alternating labels, together with one isolated inverse-labelled interval. Hence each \(P_i\) is admissible.

At a crossing of chords \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\) with
\[
i<j<k<\ell,
\]
the two resolutions are
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Therefore, over \(\mathbb F_2\),
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Thus
\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0
\]
because each \(P_i\) occurs exactly twice.

Let
\[
z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\in C_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2),
\]
with the forced caps reinserted. The three \(Q_i\) are distinct \(2\)-cells, so \(z\neq 0\).

## 4. There are no \(3\)-cells

In the skeleton there are only \(3\) intervals. Let \(N\) be any admissible skeleton matching with \(k\) crossings, and let \(c\) be the number of connected components of \(\Gamma(N)\). By Lemma 1, \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest.

The graph has
\[
V=6+k
\]
vertices: six boundary vertices and \(k\) crossing vertices. It has
\[
E=3+2k
\]
edges, because each of the three intervals contributes one edge, and each crossing cuts two interval segments, adding two edges total.

Since \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest,
\[
E=V-c.
\]
Hence
\[
3+2k=6+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=3-c\le 2.
\]
Therefore no admissible skeleton matching has \(3\) crossings, and hence \(F_w\) has no \(3\)-cells.

We are computing cellular homology of the CW complex \(F_w\); by the cellular homology theorem this agrees with singular homology. Since there are no \(3\)-cells,
\[
\operatorname{im}\partial_3=0.
\]
The nonzero \(2\)-cycle \(z\) is therefore not a boundary. Hence
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]

A contractible space has trivial homology in positive degrees, so \(F_w\) is not contractible. Thus the general answer to the original contractibility question is negative.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle$ be a free group. A word on the
  letters $L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}$ is called reducible
  if it equals $1\in G$. A word is called quasi-reduced if it does not
  contain three consecutive letters $\ell\ell^{-1}\ell$ for any

  Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word $w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}$ (with $w_i\in L$). A
  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in
  $\RR\times\RR_+$, satisfying the following:
  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of
    $\RR\times\RR_+\setminus M$, and for each component $A$ of $M\cap \bdy\overline{R}$, $\bdy A$ consists of some two points $(i,0)$ and $(j,0)$ with $w_i=w_j^{-1}$. 
  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,
  \]
  let $M_a$ be the $(k-1)$-crossing matching obtained by resolving $M$
  at $c$ according to $a$. Then the $(k-1)$-cell associated to $M_a$
  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Main Theorem (contractibility of arc and curve-system complexes)] Let S be a compact connected surface, possibly with boundary, and let V be a nonempty finite subset of S. An essential arc in (S,V) means an embedded arc meeting ∂S ∪ V only in its endpoints, whose endpoints lie in V and may coincide, and which does not separate S into two components one of which is a disk meeting V only in the endpoints of the arc. An essential circle in (S,V) means an embedded circle in S disjoint from ∂S ∪ V which does not separate S into two components one of which is either a disk meeting V in at most one point or an annulus disjoint from V. Let A(S,V) be the simplicial complex whose k-simplices are ambient-isotopy classes rel V of systems of k+1 pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs, disjoint except possibly at endpoints. Let C(S,V) be the analogous complex of systems of pairwise non-isotopic essential arcs and essential circles, mutually disjoint except possibly for arc endpoints. Excluding the trivial cases in which the complex under discussion is empty: (a) A(S,V) is contractible except when S is a disk with V contained in ∂S or when S is an annulus with V contained in one component of ∂S; in these exceptional cases A(S,V) is homeomorphic to a sphere. (b) If V contains at least one point in the interior of S, then C(S,V) is contractible.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: First prove (a) when V has at most one point on each boundary component. Choose an essential arc β. The surgery construction stated separately below gives a deformation retraction of A(S,V) onto the closed star of [β], the subcomplex of arc systems disjoint from β; a star is contractible. For general V, use the proposition that if V′ is obtained from V by adding one new vertex on a boundary component already containing a vertex, then A(S,V′) is homeomorphic to the suspension ΣA(S,V). The proof of this proposition compares both spaces by decomposing A(S,V′) into the subcomplex X of systems not containing either of two boundary-parallel arcs β,β′ adjacent to the new vertex, plus the two stars St(β), St(β′). Collapsing the boundary segment between the old and new vertex sends X into ΣA(S,V); the suspension coordinate is (θ−θ′)/2, where θ and θ′ are the total weights of arcs incident to the two adjacent vertices. The two complementary open cones are precisely the stars of β and β′, giving a PL homeomorphism. Inductively remove extra boundary vertices until each boundary component has at most one vertex. Suspension preserves contractibility, while repeated suspension of the initial S^0 cases gives spheres. The initial S^0 exceptional cases are the quadrilateral disk and the annulus with two vertices on one boundary component, yielding exactly the stated disk and annulus exceptions after adding boundary vertices. For (b), if V has an interior point, choose an essential arc β with both endpoints at that interior vertex; the same surgery deformation works for curve systems containing circles as well as arcs and retracts C(S,V) to St([β]), hence C(S,V) is contractible.
  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Surgery deformation to the star of a fixed arc (construction in the proof of the Main Theorem)] Let S be a compact connected surface and V a nonempty finite subset. Let β be an essential arc in (S,V). In the arc-only case, assume V has at most one point in each component of ∂S and set X = A(S,V). In the curve-system case, assume β has both endpoints at a point of V lying in the interior of S and set X = C(S,V). Then X admits a deformation retraction, indeed a flow which is linear on simplices in the sense described below, onto the closed star St_X([β]). This star consists of all simplices represented by curve systems disjoint from β, allowing one member of the system to be isotopic to β. The deformation fixes St_X([β]) pointwise.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: For a simplex [α0,...,αk], isotope the representing system c1 = α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk into minimal position with β: there is no disk whose boundary is an arc in β and an arc in some αi, not equal to αi ∪ β, with interior disjoint from V. The construction is independent of the chosen minimal representative because any two minimal positions are isotopic through minimal positions: take an isotopy F from one to the other, perturb F transverse to β, then simplify the 1-manifold F^{-1}(β) by cancelling critical points of its projection to the isotopy parameter. View a point P = Σ ti αi of the simplex as a weighted curve system by replacing αi with parallel copies of total thickness ti. At the crossings with β, pinch these families into one band of total thickness θ. Choose an end of β. For t ∈ [0,1], cut into this band from the chosen end through thickness tθ and redirect the two cut ends along β to that end. Discard arcs which become inessential, in particular boundary-parallel arcs as in the paper's Figure 3; in the arc-only case, the hypothesis of at most one vertex on each boundary component ensures that not all of the weighted system is discarded. Equivalently and more combinatorially, order the intersection points x1,...,xm of c1 with β from the chosen end. Slide x1 to the end of β, then discard inessential or parallel pieces, obtaining a new curve system c2 meeting β in x2,...,xm. The union c1 ∪ c2 is again a curve system and determines a simplex σ1 containing the original simplex as a face; the initial part of the deformation moves linearly along fibers of the linear projection of σ1 to the face corresponding to c2. Repeat, sliding x2, then x3, and so on, producing c3,...,cm+1 with cm+1 disjoint from β. Thus each starting simplex is crossed by a finite sequence of linear motions through simplices σ1,...,σm. The endpoint lies in St([β]). If the starting system is already disjoint from β, there are no intersections and the flow is stationary. The construction on a face is exactly the restriction of the construction on the containing simplex, since it uses the same ordered intersections with weights of omitted curves set to zero. Hence the simplexwise flows glue to a continuous global deformation retraction.
  • [arXiv:pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f Cutting-open/link identification (used in the proof of the Corollary on elementary moves)] Let τ = [α0,...,αk] be a simplex of A(S,V), represented by a system of essential arcs in a compact connected surface S with finite vertex set V. Let (S_τ,V_τ) be the possibly disconnected surface with vertices obtained by cutting S open along representatives of the arcs α0,...,αk; for disconnected surfaces, define A(S_τ,V_τ) to be the join of the arc complexes of the connected components. Then the link of τ in A(S,V) is naturally identified with A(S_τ,V_τ). Consequently, using the Main Theorem, whenever this cut-open arc complex has dimension at least one it is connected; in particular, in the corollary's application, links of simplices of codimension at least two are connected.  (from: literature_pi_math_cornell_edu_0369f947a69f)
    Proof sketch: A simplex in the link of τ is represented by an arc system γ disjoint from α0 ∪ ... ∪ αk and containing no arc isotopic rel V to any αi. Cutting S along the αi turns γ into an arc system in S_τ. Conversely, any essential arc system in S_τ glues to an arc system in S disjoint from the αi. The essentialness conditions match: an arc in the cut surface that cut off a disk with only its endpoints as vertices would glue to an inessential arc in S, while arcs parallel to the new boundary pieces created by cutting correspond precisely to arcs isotopic to one of the αi and hence are excluded from the link. Ambient isotopies rel vertices disjoint from the αi lift to isotopies in S_τ, and isotopies in S_τ descend after gluing, so the correspondence is on isotopy classes and respects disjoint unions of arcs; therefore it is a simplicial isomorphism. If S_τ has several components, an arc system is exactly a union of independent systems in the components, which is why the complex is the join of the component complexes. For connectivity, the Main Theorem implies each nonempty component arc complex is either contractible or a sphere; any such complex of positive dimension is connected, and joins are connected as soon as the total dimension is at least one. This link-connectivity is what allows a polygonal path between maximal simplices to be pushed off simplices of codimension greater than one.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 4.11] Let P be a finite bounded poset with minimum hat0 and maximum hat1. A rooted interval [u,v]_r means the interval [u,v] together with a fixed saturated chain r from hat0 to u. A chain-atom ordering assigns, for every rooted interval [u,hat1]_r, a total order on the atoms of that rooted interval, i.e. on the elements covering u; its restriction to [u,v]_r is the induced order on atoms lying below v. A recursive atom ordering (RAO), applied recursively to rooted intervals, is an atom order a1,...,at of a finite bounded rooted interval [u,v]_r such that: (R1a) for every j, [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j} has an RAO; (R1b) for j>1, in the chosen RAO of [a_j,v]_{r∪a_j}, all atoms x covering a_j that are above some earlier atom a_i with i<j precede all atoms not above any earlier atom; and (R2) for all i<j and all y with a_i<y and a_j<y, there exist k<j and z such that a_j ⋖ z, a_k<z, and z≤y. A generalized recursive atom ordering (GRAO) is defined the same way except that (R1b) is replaced by: (G1b) for every j and every x,w with a_j ⋖ x ⋖ w≤v, when the chosen GRAO of [a_j,v] is restricted to [a_j,w], either the first atom of [a_j,w] is above some earlier atom a_i with i<j, or no atom of [a_j,w] is above any earlier atom. Then P admits a GRAO if and only if P admits an RAO. More precisely, every RAO is a GRAO. Conversely, every GRAO can be transformed into an RAO by the atom reordering process: starting with a chain-atom ordering Λ, choose any linear extension of P; leave the atom order at hat0 unchanged; then, proceeding upward through the linear extension and through all roots r for [u,hat1], let u^- be the element immediately below u in r and r^- the root with u deleted. Using the already reordered atom order in [u^-,hat1]_{r^-}, put first all atoms x of [u,hat1]_r that are above some atom of [u^-,hat1]_{r^-} preceding u, in their original relative Λ-order, and then all remaining atoms, again in their original relative Λ-order. If Λ is a GRAO, the resulting chain-atom ordering Λ^re is an RAO.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: RAO ⇒ GRAO is by induction on longest chain length. Conditions (R1a) and (R2) are exactly the recursive and exchange conditions needed for GRAO. For (G1b), use that an RAO gives a CL-labeling and CL-labelings restrict to intervals; converting the restricted CL-labeling back to an RAO shows the RAO restricted to [a_j,w] still has the all earlier-overlap atoms first property, which implies the weaker GRAO alternative. For GRAO ⇒ RAO the proof builds a controlled reordering. Key preliminary facts: (1) a GRAO restricts to every rooted subinterval [u,v]_r; (2) the GRAO alternative extends from covers to arbitrary [a_j,v]: either the first atom is above an earlier atom or no atom is; (3) if two consecutive atoms a_i,a_{i+1} in [u,hat1]_r are never the first atom of any rooted interval [u,w]_r containing both, then swapping them preserves the GRAO. The swap lemma is the technical core: one proves the F and G sets of atoms above earlier atoms are unchanged by such a swap, then checks separately the recursive condition, the GRAO first-atom alternative, and the exchange condition. Next, the atom reordering process is shown to preserve the first atom of every rooted interval [u,v]_r. This is proved by induction on the root length. If the first atom x of [u,v]_r were moved behind another atom, then x lies in the G part. If x is above an earlier atom below u, the earliest atom in the lower interval [u^-,x] would have to move after u, contradicting induction. If no atom of [u,v]_r is above an earlier atom, then u was already first in [u^-,v] and remains first by induction, so every atom of [u,v]_r lies in G and x cannot move. Since the reordering preserves first atoms, each permutation it performs on atoms of [u,hat1]_r can be written as adjacent transpositions each preserving first atoms in all subintervals; the swap lemma implies the entire reordered chain-atom ordering remains a GRAO. Finally induct on longest chain length. Below each atom a_j, the reordered order is obtained from a GRAO on [a_j,hat1] by a first-atom-preserving initial permutation and then by atom reordering, hence is an RAO by induction. The construction itself enforces RAO condition (R1b), and because the output is still a GRAO it satisfies the common exchange condition (R2). Thus Λ^re is an RAO.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 6.4] Let P be a finite bounded poset. A chain-edge labeling labels each cover relation x⋖y with a value that may depend on the root, i.e. on the saturated chain from hat0 to x, with the usual consistency that identical initial rooted chains give identical labels on their common edges. For a rooted interval [u,v]_r, label sequences of saturated chains are compared lexicographically. A topological ascent at x⋖y⋖z means that the two-edge label sequence through y is lexicographically earliest among all saturated chains from x to z with the same root; otherwise it is a topological descent. A CL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique strictly ascending saturated chain and that chain has lexicographically first label sequence. A CC-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique saturated chain consisting entirely of topological ascents, and in each rooted interval all saturated chains have distinct label sequences with no label sequence a prefix of another. A TCL-labeling is a chain-edge labeling for which every rooted interval has a unique lexicographically earliest saturated chain and every other saturated chain has at least one topological descent. The unique earliest, or UE, property means that in every rooted interval [u,v]_r the smallest label occurring on an initial cover u⋖a, with a an atom of [u,v], occurs for exactly one such atom. A labeling with unique lexicographically earliest chains is self-consistent if whenever a is the atom on the lexicographically first saturated chain of [u,v]_r and b≠a is another atom of [u,v]_r, then for every v' with a,b≤v', every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through a is lexicographically earlier than every saturated chain of [u,v']_r through b. With RAO and GRAO as in Theorem 4.11, the following eight conditions on P are equivalent: (1) P admits an RAO; (2) P admits a GRAO; (3) P admits a CL-labeling; (4) P admits a CL-labeling with the UE property; (5) P admits a self-consistent CC-labeling; (6) P admits a CC-labeling with the UE property; (7) P admits a self-consistent TCL-labeling; (8) P admits a TCL-labeling with the UE property. Moreover, each implication is constructive: either the object satisfying one condition is already an object satisfying another, or the proof gives an explicit construction of the latter from the former.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: The proof is a web of constructive implications. (1)⇔(2) is Theorem 4.11: atom reordering turns any GRAO into an RAO, and every RAO is a GRAO. (1)⇔(3) is Björner-Wachs: finite bounded posets admitting RAOs are exactly CL-shellable posets. (3)⇔(4) uses Lemma 5.5, which proves every CL-labeling has UE. The key contradiction is: if two atoms a,a' of [u,v]_r share the minimum first label, take the lex-first chain M through a and the lex-first chain M' through a'. Since M is ascending, λ(u,a)< its next label; since M is lexicographically before M' and first labels agree, the next label on M is ≤ the next label on M'. Hence M' also ascends at a'. M' is ascending above a' because it is lex-first in [a',v], so [u,v]_r has two ascending chains, impossible. UE implies self-consistency (Lemma 5.3), because the unique atom with smallest first label beats every chain through every other atom in all larger intervals with the same root. RAOs also directly give CC-labelings with UE by labeling u⋖a_i with the index i in the recursively chosen atom order; distinct index sequences and the RAO exchange axiom force every non-first chain to have a topological descent. Conversely, a self-consistent TCL-labeling gives a GRAO (Theorem 6.3). Construct the atom order at [u,hat1]_r greedily: choose a1 from a lexicographically earliest saturated chain; after choosing a1,...,ai, choose a_{i+1} from a lexicographically earliest chain avoiding those atoms. Self-consistency makes this order compatible with lex-first choices in all subintervals. Inductively the upper intervals have GRAOs. For GRAO condition (G1b), suppose some atom of [a_j,w] is above an earlier atom but the first atom x1 of [a_j,w] is not. The lex-first chain through x1 then combines with the topological ascent hat0⋖a_j⋖x1 to give one topologically ascending chain to w, while the earlier atom below w gives another, contradicting uniqueness. For the exchange condition, if a_i<a_j both lie below y with i<j, the lex-first chain in [a_j,y] cannot remain topologically ascending from hat0, because the lex-first chain through the earlier atom already gives the unique topologically ascending chain; therefore there is a topological descent at the bottom, and the alternative two-step path supplies z and an earlier atom a_k<z with a_j⋖z≤y. CC-labelings are TCL-labelings because distinct non-prefix label sequences and uniqueness of the topologically ascending chain imply uniqueness of the lex-first chain and descents on all others. These implications give all eight equivalences, including the UE variants.
  • [arXiv:2212.03949 Theorem 7.1] For every n, let P_n be the uncrossing poset of complete matchings on 2n cyclically ordered boundary nodes, with an added minimum element hat0. The elements other than hat0 are perfect matchings, represented by strand diagrams. A noncrossing matching covers hat0. More generally, a matching u is covered by a matching v when v has rank one larger than u, where the rank is one plus the minimal possible number of strand crossings, and u is obtained from v by uncrossing a single crossing pair of strands in one of the two possible ways. The order is generated by these cover relations. Then P_n is dual CL-shellable; equivalently, the dual poset P_n^* admits a CL-labeling.  (from: literature_2212.03949)
    Proof sketch: Use the dual EC-labeling of Hersh-Kenyon. Encode a matching by numbering strands 1,...,n in the order their first endpoints are encountered clockwise from a fixed base boundary node, then reading the endpoint strand numbers clockwise; this gives a word of length 2n with two copies of each number and first occurrences increasing. If i<j and a crossing pair in v has subsequence i j i j, the two uncrossings replace it by either i j j i or i i j j. For a cover u⋖v in P_n, label the downward step v→u by (i,j) in the first case i j i j → i j j i, and by (j,i) in the second case i j i j → i i j j. Covers from noncrossing matchings down to hat0 receive a special label L. Order labels by (i,j)<L<(j,i) for i<j; among labels (i,j) with i<j use lexicographic order; among labels (j,i) with i<j use reverse lexicographic order. Hersh-Kenyon prove this is a dual EC-labeling of P_n. To upgrade to dual CL-shellability, apply the paper's Corollary 6.5, a consequence of Theorem 6.4: any self-consistent EC-labeling can be algorithmically converted to a CL-labeling. It remains to prove self-consistency, and the paper does so via the UE property. Fix an element v of P_n. The labels on all cover relations downward from v are distinct: a label K determines the two strand numbers being uncrossed, and its order, (i,j) versus (j,i), determines which of the two uncrossings was performed; this data uniquely determines the lower matching u. The special label L occurs only for the unique downward cover from a noncrossing matching to hat0. Hence, in the dual poset, all labels on covers upward from any fixed element are distinct, so in particular the smallest such label occurs uniquely. Thus the dual EC-labeling has the UE property. Lemma 5.3 gives UE ⇒ self-consistent, and Corollary 6.5 then yields a dual CL-labeling.

Let P_n be the uncrossing poset whose nonzero elements are pairings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points by n wires, with the fully crossed pairing as maximum and with an adjoined minimum 0-hat; a diagram D′ is covered by D in P_n when D′ is obtained from D by locally uncrossing one crossing pair of wires in one of the two possible ways and the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one, equivalently no pair of wires becomes double-crossed. If the cover uncrosses crossing wires k < m whose induced subword in w(D) is k,m,k,m, then the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,m,m,k has label (k,m), while the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,k,m,m has label (m,k). Covers from a noncrossing diagram to the artificial top of P_n^* are labeled L. Order labels by (i,j) with i < j lexicographically, then L, then labels (r,s) with r > s ordered by decreasing second coordinate and, for equal second coordinate, decreasing first coordinate: (n,n−1) < (n,n−2) < (n−1,n−2) < ... < (2,1). Moreover, since Lam proved P_n is Eulerian, P_n is a CW poset: for every x > 0-hat in P_n, the order complex of the open interval (0-hat,x) is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(x)−2, so P_n is the face poset of a regular CW complex with 0-hat representing the empty cell.     Proof sketch: The proof is by analyzing intervals of P_n^*. For intervals [u,v] with v below the artificial top, Lemma 4.1 shows that every ordinary label descent is already a topological descent, so a topologically ascending chain must have weakly increasing labels. If the start set S(D), the set of positions of p-endpoints in w(D), is unchanged across the interval, the map D ↦ π(D), where π(D) is the permutation obtained by reading the v-endpoints, identifies the interval with a type A Bruhat interval; under this identification λ is Dyer's reflection-order EL-labeling, so the weakly increasing chain is unique. If the start set changes, only labels (j,i) with j > i move p-endpoints. The imposed order on these labels forces the last such move: choose the smallest i whose v-end in the target either occupies a source start-position or contains nested below it a v-end occupying such a position; then the first coordinate j is determined by the word just before that position. If two different predecessors of v had the same forced label, the chain would require a lower label (t,i) and the local three-wire analysis of Proposition 4.3 would create a topological descent. Iterating determines the whole moving-start part, and the remaining fixed-start part is the Bruhat increasing chain. For intervals [u, artificial top], the lexicographically first chain is constructed greedily: choose the smallest wire i crossing some larger wire, then the smallest such j, and uncross to the resolution with subword i,j,j,i. A renaming argument shows this step cannot make a smaller label available, so the greedy label sequence is weakly increasing and ends with L. Any other saturated chain either deviates before the top, in which case the preceding interval analysis gives a topological descent, or uses a label (j,i) with j > i before the final L, which is itself a descent since (j,i) > L. Thus every interval has a unique topologically ascending chain. EC-shellability gives shellability. Finally, Lam's Eulerian theorem implies thinness, and the Björner-Danaraj-Klee criterion says a finite graded thin shellable poset with a minimum is a CW poset.
  • [arXiv:1803.06217 Lemma 3.16] Let D be a wire diagram in P_n with at least one crossing. For i < j, define the noncrossing-pair set N(D) as follows: include (i,j) in N(D) if the induced subword of w(D) on wires i and j is i,j,j,i, and include (j,i) in N(D) if that induced subword is i,i,j,j. Thus neither ordered pair is in N(D) exactly when wires i and j cross, i.e. the induced subword is i,j,i,j. Consider a candidate downward cover D′ ≺ D in P_n obtained by uncrossing a crossing pair of wires in the way whose dual P_n^* edge-label would be the ordered pair (k,m), with k ≠ m. This candidate is an actual cover relation D′ ≺ D in P_n if and only if (k,m) is not in N(D), (m,k) is not in N(D), and the following additional conditions hold: if k < m, then for every l with k < l < m one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1; if k > m, then for every l with l < m or k < l one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1.  (from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: A downward step is a cover precisely when the chosen smoothing removes the crossing of wires k and m and does not introduce a double crossing; equivalently the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one. The ordered label (k,m) specifies which of the two smoothings is being tested. Pairs of wires not involving k or m are unchanged, and k,m themselves become noncrossing, so the only possible obstruction is a third wire l that becomes double-crossed with k or m. The cyclic order constraints from the endpoint naming greatly restrict which l can matter: for k < m only k < l < m can be dangerous; for k > m only l < m or k < l can be dangerous. For each such l one checks the allowable six-letter induced subwords on the three wires k,l,m. The smoothing labeled (k,m) is harmless exactly when, before smoothing, l is noncrossing in the specified orientation with exactly one of the two ordered pairs (k,l) and (l,m). If both ordered pairs are in N(D), or neither is, the local reconnection forces l to meet one of the other two wires twice, so the resulting diagram is not a cover. The two initial requirements (k,m),(m,k) not in N(D) say exactly that k and m really cross in D. This case check is the full no-double-crossing criterion, yielding the stated equivalence.

(from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: It suffices to examine the two consecutive uncrossings from x to z. If they involve four distinct wires, the two uncrossings commute: performing them in the opposite order gives x ≺ y′ ≺ z. One then compares labels. Reversing the order preserves whether each individual label is of increasing type (a,b) with a < b or decreasing type (a,b) with a > b, because deleting unrelated wires does not change which endpoint of the two-wire pair is first. The paper checks the resulting cases from the label order: if both labels are decreasing, a descent forces the second coordinate of the first label to be smaller, making the swapped first label smaller; if the first is decreasing and the second increasing, either the increasing label is automatically smaller or its first coordinate is smaller; if both are increasing, a descent forces the second uncrossing's first coordinate to be smaller. In each case λ(x,y′) < λ(x,y). If the two uncrossings involve only three wires, restrict the diagram to those three wires, giving the corresponding rank-two situation in P_3^*. The relative order of relevant labels is preserved under this restriction and under reinclusion: increasing versus decreasing type is preserved, comparisons of minima are preserved, and ties are broken by the same comparison of maxima. A direct check of the labeled Hasse diagram of P_3^* shows every descent there has a lexicographically earlier alternate middle element. Lifting that alternate chain back to the original three wires inside P_n^* gives the required y′. The hypothesis z below the artificial top avoids the special L-labeled final cover, so all cases are covered.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Proposition 2.7] Let A be a complementary alphabet, meaning a set equipped with a fixed-point-free involution B ↦ comp(B). Let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. For a rooted plane tree with n edges, label its half-edges 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise around the tree from the root; write e(u,v) for the edge whose two half-edges have labels u and v. A plane tree is P-valid if for every edge e(u,v), the letters p_u and p_v are complementary. Fix indices i<j<i'<j'. The local moves are as follows: a type 1 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') occur, replaces them by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type 2 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') occur, replaces them by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move on a P-valid tree is valid if the resulting tree is also P-valid. Then: (1) there is a valid type 1 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). (2) there is a valid type 2 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B).  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: The combinatorial hypotheses, namely that the two relevant edges are adjacent and have indices i<j<i'<j', ensure that the stated replacement is a legal local move of plane trees. Validity is then exactly a four-letter condition. For type 1, the starting tree is P-valid, so p_i is complementary to p_j and p_{i'} is complementary to p_{j'}. The resulting tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_{j'} and p_j is complementary to p_{i'}. Let B=p_i. Since complements are unique and comp is an involution with no fixed points, the four complementary-pair requirements force p_j=comp(B), p_{j'}=comp(B), and p_{i'}=B. Conversely, if these equalities hold, then both original edges e(i,j), e(i',j') and both new edges e(i,j'), e(j,i') pair complementary letters, so the type 1 move is valid. The type 2 statement is the same argument with the initial and final pairs interchanged: initial validity of e(i,j') and e(j,i') and final validity of e(i,j), e(i',j') impose precisely the same equalities p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). The proposition is therefore an exact local label criterion for validity of the elementary move.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.4] Let A be a complementary alphabet with complement map B ↦ comp(B), and let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. Run the following greedy stack algorithm from left to right. Store indexed letters on a stack. At step j, if the stack is nonempty and its top entry is an indexed letter (i,p_i) with p_i=comp(p_j), pop that top entry and record the ordered pair (i,j). Otherwise push (j,p_j). Let E(P) be the set of pairs recorded by the algorithm. Let V(P) denote the set of P-valid plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of {1,...,2n} in which matched positions carry complementary letters. If V(P) is nonempty, then the greedy algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and the recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T_0(P) in V(P).  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: The main preliminary fact is Lemma 3.3, a block-removal property of the stack algorithm. If the algorithm pops p_i at step j, then while p_i sat on the stack all letters p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that were pushed above it must have been popped before step j. Hence the algorithm matches the whole interval {i,i+1,...,j} internally by a perfect matching, and deleting the contiguous block p_i...p_j from P leaves exactly the same stack evolution and recorded pairs outside the block. This is the LIFO/nesting mechanism that makes the greedy pairs noncrossing. To prove the theorem, assume for contradiction that Q=q_1...q_{2k} is a shortest word admitting at least one Q-valid plane tree but for which the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. A valid plane tree has a leaf edge e(s,s+1), so q_s and q_{s+1} are complementary; therefore the greedy algorithm must pop at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1; since there were no earlier pops, this pop removes q_j. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} leaves a shorter word on which the greedy algorithm has the same final stack as it has on Q. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), removing that leaf gives a valid plane tree for the shorter word, contradicting minimality of Q. It remains only to ensure the existence of such a valid tree. Take any Q-valid tree. If it does not already contain e(j,j+1), the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 occur in one of the few possible noncrossing configurations: both on left sides of adjacent edges, both on right sides of adjacent edges, or forming the peak configuration displayed in the paper. Since q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, their partners have the complementary labels needed for Proposition 2.7, so an appropriate valid local move transforms the tree into one containing the leaf e(j,j+1). Deleting that leaf gives the desired shorter valid instance, contradiction. Therefore no minimal counterexample exists; whenever a valid plane tree exists, the greedy stack empties and its nested complementary pairs define a P-valid plane tree.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.6 and Corollaries 3.8, 3.10] Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p_1...p_{2n} be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T_0(P) be the P-valid plane tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose undirected edges join two trees related by a valid local move. Let G_P^+ be the directed graph with the same vertices, with a directed edge T -> T' exactly when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T_0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+. Equivalently, T_0(P) has no outgoing valid type 2 move, and every P-valid plane tree T different from T_0(P) has at least one outgoing valid type 2 move. Moreover G_P^+ has no directed cycles, and from every P-valid plane tree T there is a directed path in G_P^+ consisting entirely of valid type 2 moves from T to T_0(P). In particular the underlying undirected graph G_P is connected.  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: First show T_0 has no valid type 2 move. If T_0 contained adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' supporting a valid type 2 move, Proposition 2.7 would give p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). In the greedy construction of T_0, by the subtree/nesting property of plane trees, all material between i and j has already been matched internally when step j is reached, so p_i is on top of the stack. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pair i with j, producing e(i,j), contradiction. Next prove every T != T_0 has a valid type 2 move. Let j be the first half-edge at which T and T_0 differ in left/right status. If j were a left half-edge in T_0 and a right half-edge in T, say in edge e(i',j), then the interval i'+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T; all its earlier half-edge statuses agree with T_0, hence the same nested matching occurs in T_0. Thus p_{i'} is on top of the greedy stack at step j and would be paired with p_j, forcing e(i',j) in T_0, contradiction. Therefore j is a right half-edge in T_0, paired there with some i<j, while i and j are both left half-edges in T. The interval i+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T_0 and, by minimality of j, the same subtree occurs in T. Thus in T the edges beginning at i and j are adjacent in precisely the configuration of a type 2 move. Since T_0 pairs i with j, p_i and p_j are complementary; P-validity of T gives complementary partners on the two adjacent edges, and Proposition 2.7 makes the type 2 move valid. This proves uniqueness of the sink. For acyclicity, use the height function d_T=sum_v dist(v,v_0), where v_0 is the root. In the schematic for a type 2 move, the move decreases this total distance by at least one, and by more if the intervening subtree denoted C in the paper is nonempty; no type 2 move can preserve or increase d_T. Hence G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Since the graph is finite and every non-T_0 vertex has an outgoing edge, following any sequence of outgoing type 2 moves must eventually terminate, and the only possible terminal vertex is the unique sink T_0. Thus every valid plane tree has a directed type 2 path to T_0. The paper also proves connectedness of G_P independently by induction: two valid trees either share an edge, allowing induction on the inside and outside subtrees, or a valid local move can first be applied to one tree to create a shared edge.
  • The region-label rule forbids lenses and interior cycles; hence any valid cell is represented by an ordinary chord diagram in which crossings are determined by endpoint interleaving.  (from: t2)

## Referee verdict

I find **no genuine gap** in the proposed counterexample, provided the final write-up makes the planar-subset convention at crossings explicit and treats \(a,A,a,A,a,A\) only as an auxiliary six-endpoint skeleton.

The counterexample word is

\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]

with uppercase letters denoting inverses. It freely reduces to \(1\), and direct inspection shows that it contains no consecutive triple \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\). The proof should then proceed through the following lemmas.

---

## Lemmas that should appear in the final proof

### Lemma 1: Literal side-arcs and the forest lemma

Let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the planar graph obtained from \(M\) by declaring marked endpoints and crossings to be vertices. For a complementary region \(R\), a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is a literal side-component of this planar subset: at a crossing it follows the two adjacent half-edges bounding the sector of \(R\). If such a component closes up, branches, or has a crossing as an endpoint, it does **not** have boundary consisting of two marked boundary points, so admissibility fails.

**Statement.** If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest. Consequently, no two intervals cross more than once, and crossing between two intervals is forced by endpoint interleaving.

**Reason.** A graph cycle lies in the open half-plane and bounds a Jordan disk containing no marked endpoints. Some complementary region inside that disk has an \(M\)-boundary component with no marked boundary endpoints, contradicting admissibility. Repeated crossings give a bigon cycle. Endpoint interleaving gives odd mod-\(2\) intersection number, and non-interleaving gives even mod-\(2\) intersection number; since repeated crossings are impossible, this determines actual crossing.

---

### Lemma 2: Side-neighbor lemma

**Statement.** Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\), with boundary leaves \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) in cyclic boundary order. For each cyclically consecutive pair \(x_i,x_{i+1}\), the unique path in \(T\) between them occurs as a side-component of some complementary region. Hence admissibility forces

\[
w_{x_i}=w_{x_{i+1}}^{-1}.
\]

Conversely, for a planar forest, all side-components arise this way.

**Reason.** For \(T\) alone, this is the standard regular-neighborhood/fatgraph description of a plane tree: each side of the tree runs along the path between consecutive leaves. Other matching components do not invalidate this: a sufficiently small one-sided collar of that side path avoids all other components, so the same path remains an \(M\)-boundary component in the full complement.

This lemma is the right repair for the most delicate point.

---

### Lemma 3: Unique inverse pairs are forced uncrossed intervals

**Statement.** If a letter \(x\) and its inverse \(X\) occur exactly once each in \(w\), then in every admissible matching the \(x\)-endpoint is paired directly with the \(X\)-endpoint by an uncrossed isolated interval.

**Reason.** In the tree component containing the \(x\)-leaf, both cyclic side-neighbors of that leaf must be labelled \(X\). Since there is only one \(X\)-leaf, the component has exactly two leaves. If a tree component has \(m\) leaves and \(v\) crossing vertices, then

\[
m+4v=2(m+v-1),
\]

so \(m=2v+2\). Thus \(m=2\) implies \(v=0\).

Applied to \(w\), the filler pairs

\[
(2,3),\ (5,6),\ (8,9),\ (11,12),\ (14,15)
\]

are forced isolated uncrossed adjacent caps.

---

### Lemma 4: Deleting the caps is a cellular identification

Because the five forced intervals are adjacent caps, each bounds an empty boundary disk and is isotopic rel boundary to a standard small cap. Deleting them leaves the six endpoints

\[
1,4,7,10,13,16
\]

with labels

\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

After relabelling these as \(1,\dots,6\), one obtains an auxiliary six-endpoint skeleton complex \(K\). Deleting and reinserting the caps gives a **cellular isomorphism** between \(F_w\) and \(K\): crossings are unchanged, and resolving a skeleton crossing commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps.

This should be stated as a cellular identification, not merely as a homotopy equivalence.

---

### Lemma 5: Six-endpoint skeleton enumeration and uniqueness

In the skeleton labelled

\[
1:a,\quad 2:A,\quad 3:a,\quad 4:A,\quad 5:a,\quad 6:A,
\]

the admissible \(2\)-cells are exactly

\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),\quad
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),\quad
Q_3=(15)(24)(36),
\]

and the admissible \(1\)-cells are exactly

\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]

Each listed endpoint pairing determines a unique isotopy class. The final write-up should explicitly justify this: endpoint interleaving fixes which pairs cross, forestness forbids repeated crossings, and Jordan separation forces the order of the two crossings along the central interval in each \(Q_i\).

There are no hidden \(3\)-cells. One clean proof is the forest degree count: for a forest with \(m=6\) leaves, \(v\) crossing vertices, and \(c\ge 1\) components,

\[
6=2v+2c,
\]

so \(v=3-c\le 2\). Thus no admissible skeleton matching can have three crossings.

---

## Homology calculation

Over \(\mathbb F_2\), the relevant cellular boundaries are

\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]

\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]

\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]

Hence every \(P_i\) appears twice in

\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3),
\]

so

\[
z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3
\]

is a nonzero mod-\(2\) \(2\)-cycle. Since there are no \(3\)-cells, \(z\) is not a boundary. Thus

\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0,
\]

so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

---

## Wording pitfalls to avoid

1. Do **not** call \(a,A,a,A,a,A\) a quasi-reduced word complex. It is only an auxiliary skeleton.
2. Do **not** split strands abstractly at crossings. Use the literal planar subset \(\Gamma(M)\).
3. Do **not** leave cap deletion as a homotopy argument; it is a cell-preserving identification.
4. Do **not** ignore possible duplicate isotopy classes; explicitly cite plane-forest uniqueness.
5. Do **not** count \((14)(25)(36)\) as a \(3\)-cell; it contains a cycle and is inadmissible.

With those clarifications, I would certify the counterexample.
