Covers from noncrossing matchings down to hat0 receive a special label L. Order labels by (i,j)<L<(j,i) for i<j; among labels (i,j) with i<j use lexicographic order; among labels (j,i) with i<j use reverse lexicographic order. Hersh-Kenyon prove this is a dual EC-labeling of P_n. To upgrade to dual CL-shellability, apply the paper's Corollary 6.5, a consequence of Theorem 6.4: any self-consistent EC-labeling can be algorithmically converted to a CL-labeling. It remains to prove self-consistency, and the paper does so via the UE property. Fix an element v of P_n. The labels on all cover relations downward from v are distinct: a label K determines the two strand numbers being uncrossed, and its order, (i,j) versus (j,i), determines which of the two uncrossings was performed; this data uniquely determines the lower matching u. The special label L occurs only for the unique downward cover from a noncrossing matching to hat0. Hence, in the dual poset, all labels on covers upward from any fixed element are distinct, so in particular the smallest such label occurs uniquely. Thus the dual EC-labeling has the UE property. Lemma 5.3 gives UE ⇒ self-consistent, and Corollary 6.5 then yields a dual CL-labeling.
  • [arXiv:1803.06217 Theorem 3.18 and Corollary 3.19] Fix n ≥ 2. Let P_n be the uncrossing poset whose nonzero elements are pairings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points by n wires, with the fully crossed pairing as maximum and with an adjoined minimum 0-hat; a diagram D′ is covered by D in P_n when D′ is obtained from D by locally uncrossing one crossing pair of wires in one of the two possible ways and the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one, equivalently no pair of wires becomes double-crossed. Let P_n^* be the dual. Choose a boundary basepoint and, for every diagram D, label endpoints clockwise as follows: the first endpoint of the i-th new wire encountered is i_p and its second endpoint is i_v; let w(D) be the word with the p/v subscripts suppressed. Define an edge-labeling λ of covers D ≺ D′ in P_n^* as follows. If the cover uncrosses crossing wires k < m whose induced subword in w(D) is k,m,k,m, then the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,m,m,k has label (k,m), while the resolution whose induced subword in w(D′) is k,k,m,m has label (m,k). Covers from a noncrossing diagram to the artificial top of P_n^* are labeled L. Order labels by (i,j) with i < j lexicographically, then L, then labels (r,s) with r > s ordered by decreasing second coordinate and, for equal second coordinate, decreasing first coordinate: (n,n−1) < (n,n−2) < (n−1,n−2) < ... < (2,1). With this labeling, every interval of P_n^* has a unique saturated chain all of whose length-two subchains are lexicographically first among the saturated chains in the corresponding rank-two interval; equivalently λ is an EC-labeling of P_n^*. Hence P_n^* is EC-shellable and P_n is shellable. Moreover, since Lam proved P_n is Eulerian, P_n is a CW poset: for every x > 0-hat in P_n, the order complex of the open interval (0-hat,x) is homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rank(x)−2, so P_n is the face poset of a regular CW complex with 0-hat representing the empty cell.  (from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: The proof is by analyzing intervals of P_n^*. For intervals [u,v] with v below the artificial top, Lemma 4.1 shows that every ordinary label descent is already a topological descent, so a topologically ascending chain must have weakly increasing labels. If the start set S(D), the set of positions of p-endpoints in w(D), is unchanged across the interval, the map D ↦ π(D), where π(D) is the permutation obtained by reading the v-endpoints, identifies the interval with a type A Bruhat interval; under this identification λ is Dyer's reflection-order EL-labeling, so the weakly increasing chain is unique. If the start set changes, only labels (j,i) with j > i move p-endpoints. The imposed order on these labels forces the last such move: choose the smallest i whose v-end in the target either occupies a source start-position or contains nested below it a v-end occupying such a position; then the first coordinate j is determined by the word just before that position. If two different predecessors of v had the same forced label, the chain would require a lower label (t,i) and the local three-wire analysis of Proposition 4.3 would create a topological descent. Iterating determines the whole moving-start part, and the remaining fixed-start part is the Bruhat increasing chain. For intervals [u, artificial top], the lexicographically first chain is constructed greedily: choose the smallest wire i crossing some larger wire, then the smallest such j, and uncross to the resolution with subword i,j,j,i. A renaming argument shows this step cannot make a smaller label available, so the greedy label sequence is weakly increasing and ends with L. Any other saturated chain either deviates before the top, in which case the preceding interval analysis gives a topological descent, or uses a label (j,i) with j > i before the final L, which is itself a descent since (j,i) > L. Thus every interval has a unique topologically ascending chain. EC-shellability gives shellability. Finally, Lam's Eulerian theorem implies thinness, and the Björner-Danaraj-Klee criterion says a finite graded thin shellable poset with a minimum is a CW poset.
  • [arXiv:1803.06217 Lemma 3.16] Let D be a wire diagram in P_n with at least one crossing. For i < j, define the noncrossing-pair set N(D) as follows: include (i,j) in N(D) if the induced subword of w(D) on wires i and j is i,j,j,i, and include (j,i) in N(D) if that induced subword is i,i,j,j. Thus neither ordered pair is in N(D) exactly when wires i and j cross, i.e. the induced subword is i,j,i,j. Consider a candidate downward cover D′ ≺ D in P_n obtained by uncrossing a crossing pair of wires in the way whose dual P_n^* edge-label would be the ordered pair (k,m), with k ≠ m. This candidate is an actual cover relation D′ ≺ D in P_n if and only if (k,m) is not in N(D), (m,k) is not in N(D), and the following additional conditions hold: if k < m, then for every l with k < l < m one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1; if k > m, then for every l with l < m or k < l one has exactly one of (k,l) and (l,m) in N(D), i.e. |{(k,l),(l,m)} ∩ N(D)| = 1.  (from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: A downward step is a cover precisely when the chosen smoothing removes the crossing of wires k and m and does not introduce a double crossing; equivalently the minimal crossing number drops by exactly one. The ordered label (k,m) specifies which of the two smoothings is being tested. Pairs of wires not involving k or m are unchanged, and k,m themselves become noncrossing, so the only possible obstruction is a third wire l that becomes double-crossed with k or m. The cyclic order constraints from the endpoint naming greatly restrict which l can matter: for k < m only k < l < m can be dangerous; for k > m only l < m or k < l can be dangerous. For each such l one checks the allowable six-letter induced subwords on the three wires k,l,m. The smoothing labeled (k,m) is harmless exactly when, before smoothing, l is noncrossing in the specified orientation with exactly one of the two ordered pairs (k,l) and (l,m). If both ordered pairs are in N(D), or neither is, the local reconnection forces l to meet one of the other two wires twice, so the resulting diagram is not a cover. The two initial requirements (k,m),(m,k) not in N(D) say exactly that k and m really cross in D. This case check is the full no-double-crossing criterion, yielding the stated equivalence.
  • [arXiv:1803.06217 Lemma 4.1] Equip P_n^* with the edge-labeling λ and label order described in Theorem 3.18: resolving crossing wires k < m to induced subword k,m,m,k has label (k,m), resolving them to k,k,m,m has label (m,k), covers to the artificial top have label L, with all (i,j), i < j, lexicographically below L and all (r,s), r > s, above L in decreasing-second-coordinate order. Let u < v < artificial top in P_n^*. If x ≺ y ≺ z is any length-two subchain occurring in any saturated chain from u to v and λ(x,y) > λ(y,z), then x ≺ y ≺ z is a topological descent: there exists another middle element y′ with x ≺ y′ ≺ z such that the label sequence (λ(x,y′), λ(y′,z)) is lexicographically smaller than (λ(x,y), λ(y,z)).  (from: literature_1803.06217)
    Proof sketch: It suffices to examine the two consecutive uncrossings from x to z. If they involve four distinct wires, the two uncrossings commute: performing them in the opposite order gives x ≺ y′ ≺ z. One then compares labels. Reversing the order preserves whether each individual label is of increasing type (a,b) with a < b or decreasing type (a,b) with a > b, because deleting unrelated wires does not change which endpoint of the two-wire pair is first. The paper checks the resulting cases from the label order: if both labels are decreasing, a descent forces the second coordinate of the first label to be smaller, making the swapped first label smaller; if the first is decreasing and the second increasing, either the increasing label is automatically smaller or its first coordinate is smaller; if both are increasing, a descent forces the second uncrossing's first coordinate to be smaller. In each case λ(x,y′) < λ(x,y). If the two uncrossings involve only three wires, restrict the diagram to those three wires, giving the corresponding rank-two situation in P_3^*. The relative order of relevant labels is preserved under this restriction and under reinclusion: increasing versus decreasing type is preserved, comparisons of minima are preserved, and ties are broken by the same comparison of maxima. A direct check of the labeled Hasse diagram of P_3^* shows every descent there has a lexicographically earlier alternate middle element. Lifting that alternate chain back to the original three wires inside P_n^* gives the required y′. The hypothesis z below the artificial top avoids the special L-labeled final cover, so all cases are covered.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Proposition 2.7] Let A be a complementary alphabet, meaning a set equipped with a fixed-point-free involution B ↦ comp(B). Let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. For a rooted plane tree with n edges, label its half-edges 1,2,...,2n by walking counterclockwise around the tree from the root; write e(u,v) for the edge whose two half-edges have labels u and v. A plane tree is P-valid if for every edge e(u,v), the letters p_u and p_v are complementary. Fix indices i<j<i'<j'. The local moves are as follows: a type 1 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') occur, replaces them by e(i,j') and e(j,i'); a type 2 move, when the adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') occur, replaces them by e(i,j) and e(i',j'). A local move on a P-valid tree is valid if the resulting tree is also P-valid. Then: (1) there is a valid type 1 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j) and e(i',j') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). (2) there is a valid type 2 local move on adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') if and only if there exists B in A such that p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B).  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: The combinatorial hypotheses, namely that the two relevant edges are adjacent and have indices i<j<i'<j', ensure that the stated replacement is a legal local move of plane trees. Validity is then exactly a four-letter condition. For type 1, the starting tree is P-valid, so p_i is complementary to p_j and p_{i'} is complementary to p_{j'}. The resulting tree is P-valid exactly when p_i is complementary to p_{j'} and p_j is complementary to p_{i'}. Let B=p_i. Since complements are unique and comp is an involution with no fixed points, the four complementary-pair requirements force p_j=comp(B), p_{j'}=comp(B), and p_{i'}=B. Conversely, if these equalities hold, then both original edges e(i,j), e(i',j') and both new edges e(i,j'), e(j,i') pair complementary letters, so the type 1 move is valid. The type 2 statement is the same argument with the initial and final pairs interchanged: initial validity of e(i,j') and e(j,i') and final validity of e(i,j), e(i',j') impose precisely the same equalities p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). The proposition is therefore an exact local label criterion for validity of the elementary move.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.4] Let A be a complementary alphabet with complement map B ↦ comp(B), and let P=p_1 p_2 ... p_{2n} be a word in A. Run the following greedy stack algorithm from left to right. Store indexed letters on a stack. At step j, if the stack is nonempty and its top entry is an indexed letter (i,p_i) with p_i=comp(p_j), pop that top entry and record the ordered pair (i,j). Otherwise push (j,p_j). Let E(P) be the set of pairs recorded by the algorithm. Let V(P) denote the set of P-valid plane trees, equivalently noncrossing perfect matchings of {1,...,2n} in which matched positions carry complementary letters. If V(P) is nonempty, then the greedy algorithm terminates with an empty stack, and the recorded pairs E(P) form a P-valid plane tree T_0(P) in V(P).  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: The main preliminary fact is Lemma 3.3, a block-removal property of the stack algorithm. If the algorithm pops p_i at step j, then while p_i sat on the stack all letters p_{i+1},...,p_{j-1} that were pushed above it must have been popped before step j. Hence the algorithm matches the whole interval {i,i+1,...,j} internally by a perfect matching, and deleting the contiguous block p_i...p_j from P leaves exactly the same stack evolution and recorded pairs outside the block. This is the LIFO/nesting mechanism that makes the greedy pairs noncrossing. To prove the theorem, assume for contradiction that Q=q_1...q_{2k} is a shortest word admitting at least one Q-valid plane tree but for which the greedy algorithm ends with a nonempty stack. A valid plane tree has a leaf edge e(s,s+1), so q_s and q_{s+1} are complementary; therefore the greedy algorithm must pop at least once. Let the first pop occur at step j+1; since there were no earlier pops, this pop removes q_j. By Lemma 3.3, deleting q_j q_{j+1} leaves a shorter word on which the greedy algorithm has the same final stack as it has on Q. If some Q-valid tree has the leaf e(j,j+1), removing that leaf gives a valid plane tree for the shorter word, contradicting minimality of Q. It remains only to ensure the existence of such a valid tree. Take any Q-valid tree. If it does not already contain e(j,j+1), the two consecutive half-edges j and j+1 occur in one of the few possible noncrossing configurations: both on left sides of adjacent edges, both on right sides of adjacent edges, or forming the peak configuration displayed in the paper. Since q_j and q_{j+1} are complementary, their partners have the complementary labels needed for Proposition 2.7, so an appropriate valid local move transforms the tree into one containing the leaf e(j,j+1). Deleting that leaf gives the desired shorter valid instance, contradiction. Therefore no minimal counterexample exists; whenever a valid plane tree exists, the greedy stack empties and its nested complementary pairs define a P-valid plane tree.
  • [arXiv:1501.03238 Theorem 3.6 and Corollaries 3.8, 3.10] Let A be a complementary alphabet, let P=p_1...p_{2n} be a word in A with at least one P-valid plane tree, and let T_0(P) be the P-valid plane tree produced by the greedy algorithm. Let G_P be the graph whose vertices are the P-valid plane trees and whose undirected edges join two trees related by a valid local move. Let G_P^+ be the directed graph with the same vertices, with a directed edge T -> T' exactly when T' is obtained from T by a valid local move of type 2. Then T_0(P) is the unique sink of G_P^+. Equivalently, T_0(P) has no outgoing valid type 2 move, and every P-valid plane tree T different from T_0(P) has at least one outgoing valid type 2 move. Moreover G_P^+ has no directed cycles, and from every P-valid plane tree T there is a directed path in G_P^+ consisting entirely of valid type 2 moves from T to T_0(P). In particular the underlying undirected graph G_P is connected.  (from: literature_1501.03238)
    Proof sketch: First show T_0 has no valid type 2 move. If T_0 contained adjacent edges e(i,j') and e(j,i') with i<j<i'<j' supporting a valid type 2 move, Proposition 2.7 would give p_i=p_{i'}=B and p_j=p_{j'}=comp(B). In the greedy construction of T_0, by the subtree/nesting property of plane trees, all material between i and j has already been matched internally when step j is reached, so p_i is on top of the stack. Since p_j is its complement, the greedy algorithm would pair i with j, producing e(i,j), contradiction. Next prove every T != T_0 has a valid type 2 move. Let j be the first half-edge at which T and T_0 differ in left/right status. If j were a left half-edge in T_0 and a right half-edge in T, say in edge e(i',j), then the interval i'+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T; all its earlier half-edge statuses agree with T_0, hence the same nested matching occurs in T_0. Thus p_{i'} is on top of the greedy stack at step j and would be paired with p_j, forcing e(i',j) in T_0, contradiction. Therefore j is a right half-edge in T_0, paired there with some i<j, while i and j are both left half-edges in T. The interval i+1,...,j-1 is a subtree of T_0 and, by minimality of j, the same subtree occurs in T. Thus in T the edges beginning at i and j are adjacent in precisely the configuration of a type 2 move. Since T_0 pairs i with j, p_i and p_j are complementary; P-validity of T gives complementary partners on the two adjacent edges, and Proposition 2.7 makes the type 2 move valid. This proves uniqueness of the sink. For acyclicity, use the height function d_T=sum_v dist(v,v_0), where v_0 is the root. In the schematic for a type 2 move, the move decreases this total distance by at least one, and by more if the intervening subtree denoted C in the paper is nonempty; no type 2 move can preserve or increase d_T. Hence G_P^+ has no directed cycles. Since the graph is finite and every non-T_0 vertex has an outgoing edge, following any sequence of outgoing type 2 moves must eventually terminate, and the only possible terminal vertex is the unique sink T_0. Thus every valid plane tree has a directed type 2 path to T_0. The paper also proves connectedness of G_P independently by induction: two valid trees either share an edge, allowing induction on the inside and outside subtrees, or a valid local move can first be applied to one tree to create a shared edge.
  • The region-label rule forbids lenses and interior cycles; hence any valid cell is represented by an ordinary chord diagram in which crossings are determined by endpoint interleaving.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Two repeated crossings or any interior cycle enclose a disk containing a complementary region whose M-boundary has no boundary endpoints, contradicting the region-label rule.
  • For an ordinary chord matching, validity is equivalent to the crossing graph being a forest and every connected component having cyclically consecutive endpoint labels inverse to one another.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: A forest crossing graph gives a planar tree component; the sides of a regular neighborhood connect consecutive boundary leaves of that component, exactly producing the stated cyclic inverse-label test.
  • For the separated word a b b^{-1} a^{-1} c c^{-1} a d d^{-1} a^{-1} e e^{-1} a f f^{-1} a^{-1}, the complex F_w is homeomorphic to S^2 and therefore is not contractible.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: The unique filler pairs are forced and uncrossed, reducing to six alternating a/a^{-1} endpoints. The resulting CW complex has five vertices, six edges, and three square 2-cells forming the standard three-square cellulation of S^2.
  • In the two-crossing ordinary forest model, there is no missing-square phenomenon: if all four one-crossing facets are valid, then the two-crossing square is valid.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: For two crossings the crossing graph is either two disjoint edges or a path; in both cases the inverse-label side conditions of the two-crossing tree are already forced by its valid one-crossing facets.
  • 0-cells of F_w are exactly noncrossing inverse-letter foldings of w.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: With no crossings, every component of M intersected with a region boundary is just an arc of the matching, so the region-label condition is exactly inverse pairing.
  • For a 1-crossing matching with crossing endpoints i<j<k<l, the region-label condition is equivalent to w_i,w_j,w_k,w_l alternating as x,x^{-1},x,x^{-1}; the two facets are the resolutions (i,j),(k,l) and (i,l),(j,k).  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: The four local side paths at the crossing have endpoint pairs (i,j),(j,k),(k,l),(i,l), giving precisely the alternating-label condition.
  • Every facet obtained by resolving one crossing of an admissible cell is again admissible.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Locally, smoothing a crossing rounds two old side-turns; every new side is an old side with the crossing rounded, so its endpoint labels remain inverse.
  • The underlying planar graph of an admissible matching is a forest.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Any graph cycle lies away from the real-line boundary and encloses a complementary region whose graph-boundary component has no real-line endpoints, contradicting admissibility.
  • There exists a reducible quasi-reduced word w for which F_w has H_2(F_w) congruent to Z, hence F_w is not contractible.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: For w=a b B A c C a d D A e E a f F A, the five separator pairs are forced uncrossed arcs, so F_w is isomorphic to F_{aAaAaA}; the latter has 5 vertices, 6 edges, 3 square 2-cells, and H_2 is Z.
  • Candidate final result: the original contractibility statement is false; the word w = a b B A c C a d D A e E a f F A gives nonzero H_2(F_w; F_2).  (from: advisor_r2)
    Proof sketch: The letters b,c,d,e,f and their inverses occur uniquely, forcing the adjacent pairs bB,cC,dD,eE,fF to be uncrossed in every admissible matching. The remaining six endpoints carry labels a,A,a,A,a,A. Three valid two-crossing matchings on these six endpoints give square 2-cells whose mod-2 boundaries cancel. Since admissible diagrams have forest crossing graph, among the three non-forced intervals there can be at most two crossings, so there are no 3-cells. The nonzero 2-cycle is therefore not a boundary.
  • For any admissible matching, the subdivided crossing graph is a forest; in the counterexample word, the five unique filler pairs bB, cC, dD, eE, fF are forced uncrossed arcs.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: A graph cycle would enclose a complementary region whose boundary side has no real-line endpoints. A unique inverse endpoint must be both side-neighbors of its mate, forcing a two-leaf tree, hence a single uncrossed interval.
  • After deleting the forced filler arcs from w, every cell has at most two crossings.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: Only three skeleton intervals remain. A forest diagram with m intervals has at most m-1 crossings, so here the crossing number is at most 2.
  • The skeleton 2-cells Q1=(13)(25)(46), Q2=(14)(26)(35), and Q3=(15)(24)(36) are valid, and their mod-2 cellular boundaries sum to zero.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: Each Qi is a tree chord diagram on the alternating labels a,A,a,A,a,A, so all side pairs have inverse labels. Explicit smoothing shows the six one-crossing facets P1 through P6 each occur in exactly two square boundaries.
  • The original question has answer no: for w=a b B A c C a d D A e E a f F A, the complex F_w has nonzero H_2 with F_2 coefficients and is not contractible.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: The chain Q1+Q2+Q3 is a nonzero 2-cycle, and there are no 3-cells in the full complex to bound it.
  • The word w = a b B A c C a d D A e E a f F A is reducible and quasi-reduced.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: Free cancellation of bB, cC, dD, eE, fF leaves aA aA aA, which reduces to 1; the only adjacent inverse pairs are those filler pairs, and none is followed by the same initial letter.
  • The planar graph of any admissible matching is a forest, so repeated crossings and lenses are impossible.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: A graph cycle lies in the open half-plane and bounds a disk; a complementary region inside gives a component of M intersected with the region boundary having no real-line endpoints, contradicting the definition.
  • The unique filler pairs in w are forced uncrossed boundary-parallel arcs, so deleting them reduces the cell problem to the six-endpoint skeleton a A a A a A.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: A unique inverse occurrence forces the two endpoints to be joined in a tree component with exactly two leaves, hence by an uncrossed interval; adjacency makes each such interval boundary-parallel.
  • In the six-endpoint skeleton, the purported third square (15)(24)(36) is not a 2-cell.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: All three endpoint pairs interleave, so the three intervals have three unavoidable crossings; the resulting crossing graph is a triangle and is inadmissible.
  • For this word, H_2(F_w; F_2)=0.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: After deleting forced arcs, there are only two valid 2-cells P and Q; their mod-2 boundaries are linearly independent, and there are no 3-cells.
  • The t3 objection to the proposed third square Q3=(15)(24)(36) is erroneous.  (from: advisor_r3)
    Proof sketch: For two chords (i,j) and (k,l), with i<j and k<l, a crossing is forced exactly when the endpoints interleave: i<k<j<l or k<i<l<j. In Q3, the chords (15) and (24) are nested because 1<2<4<5, hence they do not cross. The crossings are only (15) with (36) and (24) with (36), so Q3 is a genuine 2-crossing matching with crossing graph a path.
  • The conclusion H2=0 for the displayed padded word is unsupported because it omitted the valid third 2-cell Q3.  (from: advisor_r3)
    Proof sketch: Once Q3 is restored, the three 2-cells Q1=(13)(25)(46), Q2=(14)(26)(35), and Q3=(15)(24)(36) have boundaries in which the six one-crossing facets occur pairwise, giving the intended mod-2 2-cycle.
  • The t3 objection to Q3 is incorrect: in Q3=(15)(24)(36), the chords (15) and (24) are nested and do not cross, so Q3 has exactly two crossings.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Using the stated chord criterion, 1<2<4<5 is nesting, not interleaving. The only crossings in Q3 are (15) with (36), and (24) with (36).
  • The word w=a b B A c C a d D A e E a f F A is reducible and quasi-reduced.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Cancelling bB, cC, dD, eE, fF leaves aAaAaA, which reduces to 1. The only adjacent inverse pairs are the filler pairs, and none is followed by the same initial letter.
  • The filler pairs bB, cC, dD, eE, fF are forced uncrossed boundary-parallel arcs and can be deleted, leaving the six-endpoint skeleton a,A,a,A,a,A.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: The forest lemma implies every matching graph is a forest. A uniquely occurring inverse pair must form a two-leaf tree component, hence an uncrossed interval; adjacency makes each such interval boundary-parallel.
  • The three skeleton matchings Q1=(13)(25)(46), Q2=(14)(26)(35), and Q3=(15)(24)(36) are valid 2-cells whose mod-2 boundaries sum to zero.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Each Qi is a connected tree with leaves 1 through 6 and alternating labels, so all side pairs are inverse-labelled. Explicit smoothing gives facets P1 through P6, each appearing exactly twice in the sum of boundaries.
  • For this w, H_2(F_w; F_2) is nonzero, and therefore F_w is not contractible.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: The cycle Q1+Q2+Q3 is nonzero in C2. After deleting the forced arcs, only three intervals remain, and the forest bound gives at most two crossings, so C3=0 and the cycle cannot bound.
  • For any admissible matching, the subdivided crossing graph Γ(M) is a forest; hence repeated crossings and crossing-graph cycles are impossible.  (from: t6)
    Proof sketch: A graph cycle is a Jordan curve in the open upper half-plane. The disk it bounds contains a complementary region whose M-boundary has no real-line endpoints, contradicting the admissibility side rule.
  • For a tree component T with boundary leaves x1<...<xm, every side component of T connects cyclically consecutive leaves.  (from: t6)

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Missing-square counterexample: The small-case compatibility lemma fills every combinatorial square whose four one-crossing facets are valid.
  • Detecting noncontractibility via H1: In the separated six-endpoint skeleton the three valid 2-cells kill H1 completely; the obstruction is nonzero H2.
  • Greedy discrete Morse collapse to the greedy folding: The quasi-reduced counterexample has nonzero H_2, so no collapse to a single vertex can exist.
  • Use quasi-reducedness to exclude overlapping local moves: Quasi-reducedness excludes only literal adjacent subwords ell ell^{-1} ell; forced adjacent cancellations can pad the same obstruction into a quasi-reduced word.
  • Prove contractibility by a global greedy collapse or local-move complex argument.: The padded six-endpoint alternating skeleton produces an explicit nonzero H_2 class, so no collapse to a point can exist in general.
  • General contractibility for all quasi-reduced words: The exhibited quasi-reduced reducible word has a nonzero mod-2 H_2 class, so no such general theorem can hold.
  • Claimed S^2 counterexample from three skeleton squares: The third proposed square is actually a three-crossing triangle and violates the no-cycle condition.
  • Repair within the six-endpoint skeleton: The only endpoint pairing that could fill the missing outer square is (15)(24)(36), but its third crossing is topologically forced.
  • Reject the three-square counterexample by claiming Q3 has a triangular crossing graph.: This misclassifies the nested pair (15),(24) as crossing; the crossing graph of Q3 is a path, not a triangle.
  • t3 three-crossing invalidation of Q3: It misclassified the nested endpoint order 1<2<4<5 as a crossing; the correct chord criterion shows no crossing between (15) and (24).
  • Straight-line chord reduction: It was unnecessary and would require an extra straightening theorem; the proof was instead made directly in the embedded planar graph.
  • Counterexample via a hidden cycle with branches: Branches cannot save a graph cycle, because the Jordan disk inside the cycle still contains a complementary region whose M-boundary has no real-line endpoints.
  • Reject the counterexample by claiming Q3 has three crossings.: This misclassifies a nested pair of chords, (15) and (24), as crossing.
  • Prove contractibility by a global greedy collapse.: The audited counterexample has nonzero mod-2 H2, so no such collapse can exist in general.
  • Treating (14)(25)(36) as an admissible 3-cell: Although it has three crossings, its three chords pairwise cross, producing a crossing-graph cycle and violating the forest condition.
  • Use branches at crossings to defeat the forest lemma: Branches not adjacent to the chosen inside complementary region are not part of its boundary component, and adjacent branches still do not provide real-line endpoints.
  • Let other matching components inside a face change the side-neighbor pairs of a tree component: Other components are disjoint from the tree, so a small collar of each tree side path remains in one complementary region and the same side path remains a boundary component.
  • Find a hidden 3-cell involving the original eight intervals: The five filler intervals are forced isolated uncrossed caps, so all crossings occur among the remaining three intervals; the forest count bounds the crossing number by two.
  • Invalidate Q3 by counting three crossings: The pair (15),(24) is nested, not interleaving, so Q3 has exactly two crossings.
  • Invalidate Q3 by claiming it has three crossings.: The pair (15),(24) is nested because 1<2<4<5, so it does not cross; Q3 has exactly two crossings.
  • Find a hidden 3-cell using the original eight intervals.: The five filler intervals are forced isolated uncrossed caps, so all crossings occur among the remaining three skeleton intervals, where the forest bound gives at most two crossings.
  • Use the three-crossing matching (14)(25)(36) as a 3-cell.: Its three chords pairwise cross, giving a triangular crossing graph, which violates the forest lemma.
  • Use multiple crossings between the same two intervals to create extra cells: Two crossings between the same pair create a bigon cycle in the subdivided matching graph, contradicting the forest lemma.
  • Reverse the crossing order in a Q_i by using wiggly embedded intervals: Jordan separation and nesting force the central interval to meet the two other intervals in the standard order.
  • Treat the skeleton as an ordinary quasi-reduced word complex F_{aAaAaA}: The skeleton word aAaAaA is not quasi-reduced; the minimal repair is to regard it only as an auxiliary labelled skeleton complex identified with the padded complex after deleting forced caps.
  • Use wiggly/nonstandard embeddings to change the boundary facets or create extra cells.: For the relevant forest diagrams, planar separation and fixed endpoint data force a unique isotopy class; repeated crossings or cycles would violate the region-boundary rule.
  • Treat the auxiliary skeleton a,A,a,A,a,A as itself an instance of the quasi-reduced word problem.: That skeleton contains the forbidden pattern a A a, so it must only be used after proving a cell-preserving reduction from the genuinely quasi-reduced padded word.
  • Interpret crossings as abstractly split strands: That is not the literal topology of the subset M; with crossings as vertices, boundary components follow side-arcs through crossings.
  • Use repeated crossings or the all-pairwise-crossing skeleton as hidden higher cells: Both create graph cycles, hence complementary boundary components without marked real-line endpoints.
  • Let other components inside a face change the side-neighbor labels: A small collar of the tree side path avoids all other components, so the same side path remains a separate M-boundary component.
  • Find nonstandard isotopy classes of the named skeleton cells: For three intervals, endpoint interleaving plus separation forces both the crossing pairs and the order of crossings along each interval.

## Referee verdict

I find **no remaining fatal flaw** in the counterexample, under the literal planar-subset interpretation of admissibility. The argument should be certifiable, provided the final write-up states the topology at crossings and the cap-deletion step with sufficient precision.

The one place I would insist on tightening is the **forest lemma**: do not merely say that a cycle contains no marked endpoints because marked endpoints have degree \(1\). Instead, explicitly note that a graph cycle is compactly contained in the open half-plane, so the real boundary line lies in the unbounded complementary component; hence its Jordan disk contains no marked boundary points.

## Point-by-point audit

### 1. Graph cycles really violate admissibility

This is sound. If \(\Gamma(M)\) has a simple cycle \(C\), then \(C\) lies in the open half-plane. Since \(C\) is compact, it has positive distance from the boundary line, so the bounded Jordan disk \(D\) enclosed by \(C\) contains no marked boundary endpoints. Some complementary region inside \(D\) has an \(M\)-boundary component contained entirely in \(D\). That component either closes up, branches, or has crossing vertices as boundary; in any case its boundary is not two marked inverse-labelled endpoints. Thus admissibility fails.

This proves \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest. Repeated crossings give a bigon cycle, and endpoint interleaving then determines exactly which pairs of intervals cross.

### 2. Side-neighbor lemma with multiple components

This remains valid. For a tree component \(T\), take a sufficiently small regular neighborhood. Its side arcs are exactly the paths in \(T\) between cyclically consecutive leaves. Other components of the matching are compact and disjoint from \(T\), so the one-sided collar of any such side path can be chosen disjoint from all other components. Therefore the same side path appears as a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) for the full matching.

The final proof should state both directions: for a planar forest, all side-components arise from such consecutive-leaf paths, and admissibility is equivalent to inverse labels on every such consecutive pair.

### 3. Unique inverse pairs are forced isolated uncrossed intervals

This is sound. If \(x\) and \(X\) each occur exactly once, then in the tree component containing the \(x\)-leaf, both cyclic side-neighbors of that leaf must be labelled \(X\). Since there is only one \(X\)-leaf, the component has exactly two leaves. For a tree component with \(m\) leaves and \(v\) crossing vertices,

\[
m+4v=2(m+v-1),
\]

so \(m=2v+2\). Thus \(m=2\) implies \(v=0\), so the component is a single uncrossed interval joining the unique \(x\)- and \(X\)-endpoints.

### 4. Adjacent forced caps can be deleted cellularly

This is sound, but the final proof should phrase it as a **cellular isomorphism**, not merely a deformation or homotopy argument.

For the word

\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]

the pairs

\[
(2,3),\ (5,6),\ (8,9),\ (11,12),\ (14,15)
\]

are forced isolated uncrossed intervals. Since each pair is adjacent on the boundary, each cap bounds an empty boundary disk and is isotopic rel boundary to a standard small cap. Cutting off those empty disks and relabelling the remaining endpoints gives the auxiliary six-endpoint skeleton

\[
1:a,\quad 2:A,\quad 3:a,\quad 4:A,\quad 5:a,\quad 6:A.
\]

Deletion and reinsertion preserve crossing sets, and for every crossing \(c\),

\[
\text{delete}(M_c)=\text{delete}(M)_c.
\]

Hence attaching maps commute with deletion/reinsertion.

### 5. Skeleton enumeration: no hidden duplicates or hidden 3-cells

The listed skeleton cells are correct:

\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),\quad
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),\quad
Q_3=(15)(24)(36)
\]

are exactly the admissible \(2\)-cells, and

\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34)
\end{aligned}
\]

are exactly the admissible \(1\)-cells.

There are no hidden duplicate isotopy classes: in a forest, endpoint interleaving fixes which intervals cross, and in the two-crossing cases the order of the two crossings along the central interval is forced by Jordan separation. Thus each listed endpoint pairing gives one isotopy class.

There are no hidden \(3\)-cells. More generally, for a forest with \(m=6\) leaves, \(v\) crossing vertices, and \(c\ge 1\) connected components,

\[
6=2v+2c,
\]

so \(v=3-c\le 2\). Hence no admissible six-endpoint skeleton matching can have three crossings.

### 6. Boundary table over \(\mathbb F_2\)

The boundary table is correct. Resolving a crossing between \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<j<k<\ell\), gives

\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]

Thus over \(\mathbb F_2\),

\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]

\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]

\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]

Therefore

\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0,
\]

since every \(P_i\) occurs twice. The chain \(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\) is nonzero, and because there are no \(3\)-cells, it is not a boundary. Hence

\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0,
\]

so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

## Statements that must appear in the final write-up

1. The word \(w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A\) is reducible and quasi-reduced.

2. Crossings are interpreted literally as \(4\)-valent vertices of the planar subset \(\Gamma(M)\); side-components of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) turn through adjacent half-edges at crossings.

3. Every admissible \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.

4. In a tree component, side-components are exactly paths between cyclically consecutive leaves; admissibility forces inverse labels on those consecutive leaves.

5. A uniquely occurring inverse pair is forced to be a single isolated uncrossed interval.

6. The five filler pairs in \(w\) are forced adjacent caps, and deleting/reinserting them gives a cellular isomorphism with the six-endpoint auxiliary skeleton.

7. The skeleton has exactly the listed \(Q_i\) and \(P_i\) cells, no duplicate isotopy classes, and no \(3\)-cells.

8. The mod-\(2\) boundary table above holds, giving a nonzero class in \(H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\).

# Original Problem
Let $G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle$ be a free group. A word on the
  letters $L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}$ is called reducible
  if it equals $1\in G$. A word is called quasi-reduced if it does not
  contain three consecutive letters $\ell\ell^{-1}\ell$ for any
  $\ell \in L$.

  Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word $w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}$ (with $w_i\in L$). A
  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in
  $\RR\times\RR_+$, satisfying the following:
  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of
    $\RR\times\RR_+\setminus M$, and for each component $A$ of $M\cap \bdy\overline{R}$, $\bdy A$ consists of some two points $(i,0)$ and $(j,0)$ with $w_i=w_j^{-1}$. 
  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,
  \]
  let $M_a$ be the $(k-1)$-crossing matching obtained by resolving $M$
  at $c$ according to $a$. Then the $(k-1)$-cell associated to $M_a$
  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

# Claim Being Verified
With the listed routine patches inserted, the proof establishes that for
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A
\]
the word is reducible and quasi-reduced, the complex \(F_w\) has no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher, and the three \(2\)-cells \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) form a nonzero mod-\(2\) cellular \(2\)-cycle. Hence \(H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0\), so this \(F_w\) is not contractible. This gives a valid counterexample to general contractibility.

# Solution
**Claim.** Let \(G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle\), and write
\[
A=a^{-1},\quad B=b^{-1},\quad C=c^{-1},\quad D=d^{-1},\quad E=e^{-1},\quad F=f^{-1}.
\]
For
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]
the word \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced. Moreover, the associated complex \(F_w\) has no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher, and \(F_w\) contains three distinct \(2\)-cells \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) whose sum is a nonzero cellular \(2\)-cycle over \(\mathbb F_2\). Hence
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0,
\]
so \(F_w\) is not contractible.

Proof. First, \(w\) freely reduces to \(1\). Indeed, cancelling the adjacent pairs
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF
\]
leaves
\[
aA\,aA\,aA,
\]
which freely reduces to \(1\). Thus \(w=1\in G\).

The only adjacent inverse pairs appearing in \(w\) are
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]
They are followed respectively by
\[
A,\quad a,\quad A,\quad a,\quad A,
\]
never by the first letter of the adjacent inverse pair. Therefore \(w\) contains no subword of the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\). Hence \(w\) is quasi-reduced.

We now use a disk model. Compactify the closed upper half-plane by adding the point at infinity to its boundary, obtaining a closed disk \(D\). The marked endpoints \(1,\dots,16\) lie on \(\partial D\) in cyclic order. For a matching \(M\), let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the finite graph whose vertices are the boundary endpoints together with the crossings of \(M\), and whose edges are the arc-segments between consecutive vertices.

Call a matching admissible if it satisfies the region condition in the definition of \(F_w\).

**Planar facts.** If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest. Indeed, if \(\Gamma(M)\) contained a graph cycle, that cycle would be an embedded simple closed curve in the interior of \(D\), because boundary endpoints have degree \(1\). Let \(\Delta\subset \operatorname{int}(D)\) be the disk bounded by that curve. A component of \(\Delta\setminus M\) is contained in a complementary region \(R\) of \(D\setminus M\) whose closure misses all marked boundary points. Then some component of \(M\cap \partial\overline R\) has no marked boundary endpoints, contradicting admissibility.

Now let \(T\) be a tree embedded in \(D\), with boundary leaves \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) in cyclic order on \(\partial D\). The side paths of \(T\) are the unique paths in \(T\) joining cyclically consecutive leaves:
\[
x_1x_2,\ x_2x_3,\dots,\ x_{m-1}x_m,\ x_mx_1.
\]
If \(m=2\), the same underlying path occurs on both sides.

For such a tree \(T\), the components of \(D\setminus T\) correspond exactly to the boundary arcs between cyclically consecutive leaves, and the \(T\)-part of the boundary of the corresponding component is precisely the side path between those leaves. This follows from Euler characteristic: if \(T\) has \(v\) vertices and \(m\) boundary leaves, then \(T\cup \partial D\) has \(v\) vertices and \((v-1)+m\) edges, so it has \(m+1\) faces in the sphere; one is outside \(D\), leaving \(m\) components of \(D\setminus T\). Each component must meet \(\partial D\), otherwise its boundary would give a cycle in \(T\).

We shall use the following criterion.

**Side-path criterion.** Suppose \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest. Then \(M\) is admissible if and only if every side path of every tree component of \(\Gamma(M)\) joins boundary leaves with inverse labels.

Necessity follows as follows. Let \(P\) be a side path of a tree component \(T\). The path \(P\) is the \(T\)-boundary of some component of \(D\setminus T\). Since \(M\setminus T\) is compact and disjoint from \(P\), a sufficiently thin one-sided collar of \(P\) avoids \(M\setminus T\). That collar lies in a single component \(R\) of \(D\setminus M\), and locally at crossings its boundary follows exactly \(P\). Hence \(P\) is a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\). By admissibility, the endpoints of \(P\) have inverse labels.

Conversely, suppose every side path of every tree component has inverse-labelled endpoints. Let \(R\) be a component of \(D\setminus M\), and let \(A\) be a component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\). The set \(A\) lies in a unique tree component \(T\) of \(\Gamma(M)\). Choose a point of \(A\) in the interior of an edge. On the side of \(T\) containing \(R\), the preceding description identifies the relevant \(T\)-boundary with a side path \(P\). Again, a thin one-sided collar of \(P\) avoids \(M\setminus T\), so \(P\) lies in \(\partial\overline R\), and locally at crossings \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) follows exactly \(P\). Thus \(A=P\). By hypothesis, the endpoints of \(A\) have inverse labels, proving admissibility.

Now apply this to the letters \(b,c,d,e,f\). Each of \(b,B\), \(c,C\), \(d,D\), \(e,E\), \(f,F\) occurs exactly once in \(w\). The two side paths incident to \(p\) end at the predecessor and successor of \(p\) among the leaves of \(T\). By the side-path criterion, both endpoints must be labelled \(x^{-1}\). Since \(q\) is the unique \(x^{-1}\)-labelled endpoint, \(T\) has only the two leaves \(p,q\).

\[

\]

\[
m+4t=2m+2t-2,
\]
so
\[

\]
With \(m=2\), we get \(t=0\). Thus \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by a single uncrossed interval.

Hence every admissible matching for \(w\) contains five forced uncrossed intervals joining the adjacent pairs
\[

Each such interval, together with the boundary segment between its adjacent endpoints, bounds a disk containing no other part of the matching: otherwise another interval would have to cross the forced interval or have an endpoint on a boundary segment containing no marked points. \[
1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6.

Their labels are

a,\ A,\ a,\ A,\ a,\ A.

Deleting the five forced caps gives a three-interval skeleton diagram on these six endpoints. \[

so
\[

\]

\]
\[
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),
\]
\[
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).

Their crossings are respectively
\[

\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35),
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]
Each crossing graph is connected, and with three intervals and two crossings it has
\[

\]

\[
P_1=(12)(35)(46),
\]
\[
P_2=(15)(23)(46),
\]
\[
P_3=(13)(24)(56),
\]
\[
P_4=(13)(26)(45),
\]
\[
P_5=(16)(24)(35),
\]
\[
P_6=(15)(26)(34).
\]
Each \(P_i\) has one isolated inverse-labelled pair and one four-leaf crossing tree whose cyclic leaf labels alternate between \(a\) and \(A\). Hence each \(P_i\), with the forced caps reinserted, is an admissible one-crossing matching, i.e. a \(1\)-cell of \(F_w\).

At a crossing of \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<j<k<\ell\), the two local resolutions give the pairings
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Applying this to the crossings of \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) gives the facet table
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{two-cell} & \text{crossing resolved} & \text{resulting one-cells}\\
\hline
Q_1 & (13)\times(25) & P_1,\ P_2\\
Q_1 & (25)\times(46) & P_3,\ P_4\\
\hline
Q_2 & (14)\times(26) & P_1,\ P_5\\
Q_2 & (14)\times(35) & P_4,\ P_6\\
\hline
Q_3 & (15)\times(36) & P_3,\ P_5\\
Q_3 & (24)\times(36) & P_2,\ P_6.
\end{array}

\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[

\[

  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in

  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of

  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing

  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On

  \]

  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

Each such interval, together with the boundary segment between its adjacent endpoints, bounds a disk containing no other part of the matching: otherwise another interval would have to cross the forced interval or have an endpoint on a boundary segment containing no marked points. \[
1,\ 4,\ 7,\ 10,\ 13,\ 16.
\]

\[
1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6.
\]
Their labels are
\[
a,\ A,\ a,\ A,\ a,\ A.
\]

Deleting the five forced caps gives a three-interval skeleton diagram on these six endpoints. Conversely, any such skeleton diagram extends uniquely up to isotopy by gluing back the five empty cap disks and inserting the five standard caps. Isotopies restrict and extend over the empty cap disks, so this gives a bijection on isotopy classes. Since the forced caps have no crossings and are disjoint from the skeleton, resolving a crossing commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps.

We first show that there are no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher. Let \(M\) be any admissible matching, and delete the five forced caps. The remaining skeleton has \(r=3\) intervals, say \(k\) crossings, and \(c\) connected components in its crossing graph. Since \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest, the skeleton graph is also a forest. Thus
\[
E=V-c.
\]
But
\[
V=2r+k,\qquad E=r+2k,
\]
because each crossing contributes one vertex and cuts two interval segments. Therefore
\[
r+2k=2r+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=r-c\le r-1=2.
\]
Thus every admissible matching has at most two crossings. Hence \(F_w\) has no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher.

It remains to exhibit a nonzero mod-\(2\) cellular \(2\)-cycle. Work in the six-endpoint skeleton disk. Write \((ij)\) for an embedded interval joining endpoints \(i\) and \(j\), chosen generically. Two intervals \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<j<k<\ell\), cross once; otherwise they do not cross.

Define three two-crossing skeleton matchings:
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),
\]
\[
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),
\]
\[
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]

\[
Q_1:\ (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46),
\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35),
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]

\[
V=6+2=8,\qquad E=3+2\cdot 2=7,
\]
so it is a tree. Its leaves are the six endpoints \(1,\dots,6\), whose labels alternate \(a,A,a,A,a,A\). Therefore every side path has inverse-labelled endpoints. \[
P_1=(12)(35)(46),
\]
\[
P_2=(15)(23)(46),
\]
\[
P_3=(13)(24)(56),
\]
\[
P_4=(13)(26)(45),
\]
\[
P_5=(16)(24)(35),
\]
\[
P_6=(15)(26)(34).
\]
Each \(P_i\) has one isolated inverse-labelled pair and one four-leaf crossing tree whose cyclic leaf labels alternate between \(a\) and \(A\). Hence each \(P_i\), with the forced caps reinserted, is an admissible one-crossing matching, i.e. a \(1\)-cell of \(F_w\).

At a crossing of \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\), with \(i<j<k<\ell\), the two local resolutions give the pairings
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Applying this to the crossings of \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) gives the facet table
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{two-cell} & \text{crossing resolved} & \text{resulting one-cells}\\
\hline
Q_1 & (13)\times(25) & P_1,\ P_2\\
Q_1 & (25)\times(46) & P_3,\ P_4\\
\hline
Q_2 & (14)\times(26) & P_1,\ P_5\\
Q_2 & (14)\times(35) & P_4,\ P_6\\
\hline
Q_3 & (15)\times(36) & P_3,\ P_5\\
Q_3 & (24)\times(36) & P_2,\ P_6.
\end{array}
\]
Over \(\mathbb F_2\), orientation signs disappear, so
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Therefore
\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0,
\]
because each \(P_i\) appears exactly twice. Thus
\[
z:=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3
\]
is a cellular \(2\)-cycle over \(\mathbb F_2\).

The three \(Q_i\) are distinct \(2\)-cells, since they pair the six skeleton endpoints differently. Hence \(z\neq 0\) as a cellular \(2\)-chain. Since \(F_w\) has no \(3\)-cells, \(\operatorname{im}\partial_3=0\), so this nonzero cycle is not a boundary. Therefore
\[
0\neq [z]\in H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2).
\]
Consequently \(F_w\) is not contractible.

# Original Problem
Let $G=\langle a_1,\dots,a_r\rangle$ be a free group. A word on the
  letters $L=\{a_1,a_1^{-1},\dots,a_r,a_r^{-1}\}$ is called reducible
  if it equals $1\in G$. A word is called quasi-reduced if it does not
  contain three consecutive letters $\ell\ell^{-1}\ell$ for any
  $\ell \in L$.

  Fix a reducible quasi-reduced word $w=w_1\cdots w_{2n}$ (with $w_i\in L$). A
  matching $M$ consists of $n$ intervals, each properly embedded in
  $\RR\times\RR_+$, satisfying the following:
  \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
  \item Any two intervals intersect
    transversally, and there are no triple points.
  \item $\bdy M=\{1,\dots,2n\}\times 0\subset\RR\times 0\subset
    \RR\times\RR_+$.
  \item For each component $R$ of
    $\RR\times\RR_+\setminus M$, and for each component $A$ of $M\cap \bdy\overline{R}$, $\bdy A$ consists of some two points $(i,0)$ and $(j,0)$ with $w_i=w_j^{-1}$. 
  \end{itemize}
  A $k$-crossing
  matching is a matching where the total number of self-intersections
  is $k$.

  Let $F_w$ be the following CW complex whose $k$-cells correspond to
  isotopy classes of $k$-crossing matchings. Given a $k$-crossing
  matching $M$ with crossing set $X(M)$, the associated $k$-cell is
  given by $C(M)\defeq \prod_{c\in X(M)}I_c$, where $I_c$ is an
  interval whose endpoints correspond to the two ways of locally
  resolving $M$ near $c$. The attaching maps are given as follows: On
  a facet of the form
  \[
    a\times\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d},\qquad c\in X(M),a\in \bdy I_c,
  \]
  let $M_a$ be the $(k-1)$-crossing matching obtained by resolving $M$
  at $c$ according to $a$. Then the $(k-1)$-cell associated to $M_a$
  is given by
  $C(M_a)=\prod_{d\in X(M_a)}I_d=\prod_{d\in X(M)\setminus\{c\}}I_{d}$, and the
  above facet maps to this $(k-1)$-cell canonically.

  Is $F_w$ contractible?

**Claim.** Let \(G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle\), write
\[

Then \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced, but the associated CW complex \(F_w\) satisfies
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]
In particular, \(F_w\) is not contractible.

## 1. Two planar facts about admissible matchings

Let \(H=\mathbb R\times[0,\infty)\). For a matching \(M\), let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the finite graph obtained by declaring the boundary endpoints and all crossings to be vertices, and by taking as edges the arc-segments between consecutive vertices along the matching intervals.

A matching will be called admissible if it satisfies the complementary-region inverse-label condition from the definition of \(F_w\).

### Lemma 1: admissible graphs are forests

If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.

Indeed, suppose \(\Gamma(M)\) contained a graph cycle \(\gamma\). Since boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), \(\gamma\) lies in the interior of \(H\). As an embedded simple closed curve, \(\gamma\) separates the plane. Let \(D_\gamma\) be its bounded complementary disk. The real line lies outside \(D_\gamma\).

Choose a component \(R\) of \(H\setminus M\) contained in \(D_\gamma\). Such a component exists because \(M\) is a finite graph. Since \(\gamma\subset M\), \(R\) cannot connect to the outside of \(D_\gamma\). Hence every component of \(M\cap \partial\overline R\) lies away from the real line, so its boundary cannot consist of two marked boundary points \((i,0),(j,0)\). That contradicts admissibility. Therefore \(\Gamma(M)\) has no cycles.

### Lemma 2: side-path criterion

Compactify \(H\) by adding the point at infinity, obtaining a closed disk \(\overline H\) whose boundary circle contains the marked points in cyclic order.

Let \(N\) be a matching such that \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest. Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(N)\), and let its boundary leaves be
\[
x_1,\dots,x_m
\]
in cyclic order on \(\partial \overline H\). Let \(P_i\subset T\) be the unique tree path from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\), indices modulo \(m\).

Then the components of \(N\cap \partial\overline R\) lying in \(T\), as \(R\) ranges over components of \(\overline H\setminus N\), are precisely the paths \(P_i\). Consequently, \(N\) is admissible if and only if, for every tree component \(T\), every cyclically consecutive pair of leaves of \(T\) has inverse labels.

Proof. First consider the tree \(T\) alone. Let \(J_i\) be the boundary arc from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\). For the planar graph
\[
K=T\cup \partial\overline H,
\]
if \(T\) has \(v\) vertices, then \(K\) has \(v\) vertices and \((v-1)+m\) edges. Euler’s formula gives \(m+1\) faces, one outside \(\overline H\), so \(\overline H\setminus T\) has \(m\) components.

No component of \(\overline H\setminus T\) can avoid \(\partial\overline H\), because then its boundary would be a closed walk in the tree \(T\), forcing a cycle. Hence each of the \(m\) components is incident to exactly one boundary arc \(J_i\). The component incident to \(J_i\) has \(T\)-boundary equal to the unique path \(P_i\) from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\).

Now restore the other forest components of \(N\). They are compact and disjoint from \(T\), so a sufficiently thin one-sided collar of each path \(P_i\) is disjoint from them. Thus the full complementary regions of \(\overline H\setminus N\) adjacent to \(T\) still have \(T\)-boundary exactly one of the paths \(P_i\). Therefore the only possible components of \(N\cap \partial\overline R\) lying in \(T\) are those side paths.

Thus the admissibility condition is exactly that the endpoints of every such path \(P_i\) have inverse labels.

### Lemma 3: unique inverse pairs force uncrossed caps

Suppose a label \(x\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(p\), and \(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(q\). In any admissible matching, \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by a single uncrossed interval.

Let \(T\) be the tree component containing \(p\). By the side-path criterion, both cyclic neighbors of \(p\) among the leaves of \(T\) must have label \(x^{-1}\). Since \(q\) is the only such leaf, both neighbors are \(q\). Hence \(T\) has exactly two leaves.

If \(T\) has \(m\) leaves and \(s\) crossing vertices, then all leaves have degree \(1\), all crossings have degree \(4\), and \(T\) is a tree. Thus
\[
m+4s=2E(T)=2(m+s-1),
\]
so
\[
m=2s+2.
\]
With \(m=2\), we get \(s=0\). Therefore \(T\) is a single uncrossed interval joining \(p\) to \(q\).

## 2. The word \(w\)

We have
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
In the free group,
\[
w=a(bb^{-1})a^{-1}(cc^{-1})a(dd^{-1})a^{-1}(ee^{-1})a(ff^{-1})a^{-1}=1,
\]
so \(w\) is reducible.

It is quasi-reduced. The only adjacent inverse pairs are
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF,
\]
and the following letters are respectively
\[
A,\quad a,\quad A,\quad a,\quad A,
\]
none of which repeats the first letter of the inverse pair. Hence there is no subword \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\).

By Lemma 3, every admissible matching for \(w\) contains the five forced uncrossed intervals
\[
(2,3),\quad (5,6),\quad (8,9),\quad (11,12),\quad (14,15),
\]
joining
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]

Each such interval has adjacent boundary endpoints. Since it is uncrossed, it together with the boundary segment between its endpoints bounds a disk containing no marked boundary point. No other interval can enter that disk without crossing the forced interval or having an endpoint on that boundary segment. Hence each forced interval is a unique boundary-parallel cap up to isotopy rel endpoints.

Deleting those five caps and reindexing the remaining boundary points gives a cellular bijection with the auxiliary six-point word
\[
u=a\,A\,a\,A\,a\,A.
\]
The remaining original positions
\[
1,4,7,10,13,16
\]
become skeleton positions
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6.
\]

Admissibility is preserved in both directions by the side-path criterion: non-cap tree components have the same cyclic leaf orders and labels after deletion, while each inserted cap is a two-leaf inverse-labelled tree. Crossings are unchanged, and resolving a crossing commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps. Thus \(F_w\) is cellularly isomorphic to the corresponding skeleton complex for \(u=aAaAaA\).

## 3. Three admissible \(2\)-cells in the skeleton

Work now with the six labelled boundary points
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6
\]
with labels
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

Write \((ij)(kl)(mn)\) for the standard chord diagram joining \(i\) to \(j\), \(k\) to \(l\), and \(m\) to \(n\), with two chords crossing exactly once when their endpoints interleave.

Consider the three \(2\)-crossing matchings
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),
\]
\[
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),
\]
\[
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]
Their crossings are:
\[
Q_1:\ (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46);
\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35);
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]
In each case the graph is a connected tree with six boundary leaves in cyclic order
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6,
\]
whose labels are
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]
Thus every cyclically consecutive pair has inverse labels, so by the side-path criterion each \(Q_i\) is admissible. Hence each \(Q_i\) determines a \(2\)-cell.

Now define six admissible \(1\)-cells:
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]
Each \(P_i\) consists of one four-leaf crossing tree with alternating labels, together with one isolated inverse-labelled interval. Hence each \(P_i\) is admissible.

At a crossing of chords \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\) with
\[
i<j<k<\ell,
\]
the two resolutions are
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Therefore, over \(\mathbb F_2\),
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Thus
\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0
\]
because each \(P_i\) occurs exactly twice.

Let
\[
z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\in C_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2),
\]
with the forced caps reinserted. The three \(Q_i\) are distinct \(2\)-cells, so \(z\neq 0\).

## 4. There are no \(3\)-cells

In the skeleton there are only \(3\) intervals. Let \(N\) be any admissible skeleton matching with \(k\) crossings, and let \(c\) be the number of connected components of \(\Gamma(N)\). By Lemma 1, \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest.

The graph has
\[
V=6+k
\]
vertices: six boundary vertices and \(k\) crossing vertices. It has
\[
E=3+2k
\]
edges, because each of the three intervals contributes one edge, and each crossing cuts two interval segments, adding two edges total.

Since \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest,
\[
E=V-c.
\]
Hence
\[
3+2k=6+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=3-c\le 2.
\]
Therefore no admissible skeleton matching has \(3\) crossings, and hence \(F_w\) has no \(3\)-cells.

We are computing cellular homology of the CW complex \(F_w\); by the cellular homology theorem this agrees with singular homology. Since there are no \(3\)-cells,
\[
\operatorname{im}\partial_3=0.
\]
The nonzero \(2\)-cycle \(z\) is therefore not a boundary. Hence
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]

A contractible space has trivial homology in positive degrees, so \(F_w\) is not contractible. Thus the general answer to the original contractibility question is negative.

**Claim.** Let \(G=\langle a,b,c,d,e,f\rangle\), and write
\[
A=a^{-1},\quad B=b^{-1},\quad C=c^{-1},\quad D=d^{-1},\quad E=e^{-1},\quad F=f^{-1}.
\]
For
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A,
\]
the word \(w\) is reducible and quasi-reduced, but the associated matching complex \(F_w\) satisfies
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]
Hence \(F_w\) is not contractible.

## 1. Planar facts about admissible matchings

Call a matching **admissible** if it satisfies the complementary-region inverse-label condition in the definition of \(F_w\). For a matching \(M\), let \(\Gamma(M)\) be the finite graph whose vertices are the boundary endpoints and crossings of \(M\), and whose edges are the arc-segments between consecutive vertices along the matching intervals.

Compactify the closed upper half-plane to a closed disk \(D\), with the marked boundary points in cyclic order.

### Lemma 1: admissible matching graphs are forests

If \(M\) is admissible, then \(\Gamma(M)\) has no graph cycle.

Indeed, suppose \(\Gamma(M)\) contained a simple graph cycle \(\gamma\). Boundary endpoints have degree \(1\), so \(\gamma\) lies in the interior of the disk. Thus \(\gamma\) is a simple closed curve bounding a disk \(E\subset \operatorname{int}D\). Choose a component \(R\) of \(D\setminus M\) contained in \(E\). Then every component of \(M\cap \partial\overline R\) lies entirely in the interior of \(D\), so none has boundary consisting of two marked boundary points. That contradicts admissibility. Hence \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.

### Lemma 2: side-path criterion

Let \(N\) be a matching such that \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest. Let \(T\) be a tree component of \(\Gamma(N)\), and let its boundary leaves be
\[
x_1,\dots,x_m
\]
in cyclic order along \(\partial D\). Let \(P_i\subset T\) be the unique tree path from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\), with indices modulo \(m\).

Then the components of \(N\cap \partial\overline R\) lying in \(T\), as \(R\) ranges over complementary regions of \(D\setminus N\), are exactly the paths \(P_i\). Consequently, \(N\) is admissible if and only if, for every tree component \(T\), every cyclically consecutive pair of leaves of \(T\) has inverse labels.

Proof. First consider the tree \(T\) alone. Choose a sufficiently small closed regular neighborhood \(\nu T\subset D\) such that \(\nu T\cap \partial D\) is a disjoint union of small intervals around the leaves \(x_i\). Since \(T\) is a tree, \(\nu T\) is a disk. The components of
\[
D\setminus \operatorname{int}(\nu T)
\]
are naturally indexed by the boundary arcs \(J_i\) from \(x_i\) to \(x_{i+1}\). The part of \(\partial\nu T\) facing \(J_i\) deformation-retracts onto precisely the unique tree path \(P_i\). Shrinking \(\nu T\) back onto \(T\), the component of \(D\setminus T\) incident to \(J_i\) has \(T\)-boundary exactly \(P_i\).

Now restore the other forest components of \(N\). Choose \(\nu T\) disjoint from them. A thin one-sided collar of each \(P_i\) is still disjoint from the other components, so it lies in a unique complementary region of \(D\setminus N\). Since that region is contained in the component of \(D\setminus T\) incident to \(J_i\), its intersection with \(T\) is exactly \(P_i\). Thus the side paths adjacent to \(T\) are precisely the \(P_i\).

Therefore the admissibility condition is exactly that the endpoints of every such \(P_i\) have inverse labels.

### Lemma 3: unique inverse pairs force uncrossed caps

Suppose a label \(x\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(p\), and \(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once, at a boundary point \(q\). Then in every admissible matching, \(p\) and \(q\) are joined by a single uncrossed interval.

Let \(T\) be the tree component containing \(p\). By Lemma 2, both cyclic neighbors of \(p\) among the leaves of \(T\) must have label \(x^{-1}\). Since \(q\) is the only such leaf, both neighbors are \(q\). Hence \(T\) has exactly two boundary leaves.

If \(T\) has \(m\) boundary leaves and \(s\) crossing vertices, then every leaf has degree \(1\), every crossing vertex has degree \(4\), and \(T\) is a tree. Thus
\[
m+4s=2E(T)=2(m+s-1),
\]
so
\[
m=2s+2.
\]
With \(m=2\), we get \(s=0\). Therefore \(T\) consists of a single uncrossed interval joining \(p\) to \(q\).

## 2. The word \(w\)

We have
\[
w=a\,b\,B\,A\,c\,C\,a\,d\,D\,A\,e\,E\,a\,f\,F\,A.
\]
In \(G\),
\[
w=a(bb^{-1})a^{-1}(cc^{-1})a(dd^{-1})a^{-1}(ee^{-1})a(ff^{-1})a^{-1}=1,
\]
so \(w\) is reducible.

The only adjacent inverse pairs in \(w\) are
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]
The following letters are respectively
\[
A,\quad a,\quad A,\quad a,\quad A,
\]
none of which repeats the first letter of the inverse pair. Hence no subword has the form \(\ell\ell^{-1}\ell\). Thus \(w\) is quasi-reduced.

By Lemma 3, every admissible matching for \(w\) contains the five forced uncrossed intervals
\[
(2,3),\quad (5,6),\quad (8,9),\quad (11,12),\quad (14,15),
\]
joining the unique inverse pairs
\[
bB,\quad cC,\quad dD,\quad eE,\quad fF.
\]

Because each pair of endpoints is adjacent on the boundary, each forced interval is boundary-parallel: together with the boundary segment between its endpoints, it bounds a disk containing no marked boundary point. No other interval can enter that disk without crossing the forced interval, so the disk is empty.

Therefore every admissible matching for \(w\) is obtained uniquely, up to isotopy, by inserting those five standard boundary-parallel caps into a matching on the remaining six boundary points
\[
1,4,7,10,13,16,
\]
whose labels are
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

Let \(K\) be the auxiliary CW complex defined by the same matching construction for the six-point word
\[
u=a\,A\,a\,A\,a\,A.
\]
Deleting the five forced caps gives a bijection between admissible \(k\)-crossing matchings for \(w\) and admissible \(k\)-crossing matchings for \(u\). The inverse operation inserts the five fixed standard caps. By Lemma 2, admissibility is preserved in both directions: the inserted caps are two-leaf inverse-labelled tree components, and every non-cap tree component has the same cyclic leaf order and labels before and after deletion. Crossings are unchanged, and resolving a crossing in the non-cap part commutes with deleting or reinserting the caps. Hence
\[
F_w\cong K
\]
as CW complexes.

It remains to show that \(H_2(K;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0\).

## 3. A nonzero \(2\)-cycle in the six-point complex

Work with six cyclically ordered boundary points labelled
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6
\]
with labels
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]

Write \((ij)(kl)(mn)\) for the chord diagram joining \(i\) to \(j\), \(k\) to \(l\), and \(m\) to \(n\).

Consider the three \(2\)-crossing matchings
\[
Q_1=(13)(25)(46),
\]
\[
Q_2=(14)(26)(35),
\]
\[
Q_3=(15)(24)(36).
\]
Their crossings are respectively
\[
Q_1:\ (13)\times(25),\ (25)\times(46),
\]
\[
Q_2:\ (14)\times(26),\ (14)\times(35),
\]
\[
Q_3:\ (15)\times(36),\ (24)\times(36).
\]
Each graph is connected with six leaves and two crossing vertices, hence is a tree. Its boundary leaves occur in cyclic order
\[
1,2,3,4,5,6,
\]
with alternating labels
\[
a,A,a,A,a,A.
\]
Thus every cyclically consecutive pair has inverse labels, so by Lemma 2 each \(Q_i\) is admissible. Hence each \(Q_i\) determines a \(2\)-cell of \(K\).

Now define six admissible \(1\)-cells:
\[
\begin{aligned}
P_1&=(12)(35)(46),&
P_2&=(15)(23)(46),&
P_3&=(13)(24)(56),\\
P_4&=(13)(26)(45),&
P_5&=(16)(24)(35),&
P_6&=(15)(26)(34).
\end{aligned}
\]
Each \(P_i\) consists of one four-leaf crossing tree with alternating labels, together with one isolated inverse-labelled interval. Hence each \(P_i\) is admissible by Lemma 2.

At a crossing of chords \((i,k)\) and \((j,\ell)\) with
\[
i<j<k<\ell,
\]
the two local resolutions are
\[
(i,j)(k,\ell)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(i,\ell)(j,k).
\]
Using the attaching rule for the cubical cells, and working over \(\mathbb F_2\), we obtain
\[
\partial Q_1=P_1+P_2+P_3+P_4,
\]
\[
\partial Q_2=P_1+P_4+P_5+P_6,
\]
\[
\partial Q_3=P_2+P_3+P_5+P_6.
\]
Therefore
\[
\partial(Q_1+Q_2+Q_3)=0,
\]
because every \(P_i\) appears exactly twice.

Let
\[
z=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3\in C_2(K;\mathbb F_2).
\]
The cells \(Q_1,Q_2,Q_3\) are distinct, so \(z\neq 0\).

## 4. There are no \(3\)-cells

Let \(N\) be any admissible \(k\)-crossing matching in the six-point complex \(K\). By Lemma 1, \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest. Let \(c\) be its number of connected components.

Since there are six boundary vertices and \(k\) crossing vertices,
\[
V=6+k.
\]
There are three matching intervals, and each crossing subdivides two interval segments, so
\[
E=3+2k.
\]
Because \(\Gamma(N)\) is a forest,
\[
E=V-c.
\]
Thus
\[
3+2k=6+k-c,
\]
so
\[
k=3-c\le 2.
\]
Hence \(K\) has no cells of dimension \(3\) or higher.

Thus \(\operatorname{im}\partial_3=0\), and the nonzero \(2\)-cycle \(z\) is not a boundary. Therefore
\[
H_2(K;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]
Since \(F_w\cong K\) cellularly,
\[
H_2(F_w;\mathbb F_2)\neq 0.
\]
A contractible space has trivial homology in positive degrees, so \(F_w\) is not contractible.
